2026-06-11.intro: Thursday's drop: the most expensive AI subscription is the one nobody uses. Three things to think about — plus something small for tonight.

trends.title: Half of you have AI access. Most of you aren't using it.
trends.body: Deloitte's January 2026 enterprise report flagged something blunt: under 60% of employees with access to AI tools actually use those tools in their daily workflows — nearly the same as last year. The takeaway: licenses don't create habits. People do.

slants.strategy: Your AI ROI calculations probably assume usage you're not getting. Audit actual adoption before you defend the spend.
slants.operations: Find one tool your team has access to but doesn't open. Pick the friction point — login, integration, training — and fix that one.
slants.pm: Use sprints to make AI usage explicit: 'This week, try X tool for stand-ups.' Habit beats capability.
slants.admin: If you've got AI access through your role and aren't using it, you're not alone. Pick the smallest possible first try.

steps: List every AI tool you currently have access to through work — even free ones. (Copilot? Gemini at Workspace? An enterprise Claude account?)
Mark each: "Use weekly," "Use rarely," or "Don't use."
Pick one from the 'Don't use' list and open it now.
Ask it one real question you would normally Google or ask a colleague.
Share which tool surprised you most in the group — good or bad.

trends.title: Hours saved are real — but unevenly distributed
trends.body: Recent enterprise studies converge on a number: employees who use AI well save 2–3 hours per day on routine activities. The catch: the gains skew heavily toward people who've crossed the 'I actually trust this' threshold. Trust is the throttle.

slants.strategy: Your AI ROI model is missing a variable: time-to-trust. Plan for it explicitly.
slants.operations: Track time saved on a single workflow for two weeks. You'll find your real ROI faster than any vendor case study.
slants.pm: Time savings on individual tasks don't always show up in team velocity. The bottleneck moves — find where.
slants.admin: If you're saving 30 minutes a day, that's 2.5 hours a week. Don't fill it with more tasks — use some for learning.

steps: Pick one daily task that takes 15+ minutes (drafting an email, summarizing a doc, formatting notes).
Time yourself doing it the old way today.
Tomorrow, do the same task with AI as the first draft, then edit.
Time it again. Note the actual difference, and the quality difference.
Share your before/after in the group — let's see which tasks are worth the swap.

trends.title: The Chief AI Officer is officially a thing
trends.body: 61% of enterprises now have a Chief AI Officer or equivalent role, per recent enterprise surveys. That signals something: AI strategy moved out of IT pilots and into the boardroom. Companies with dedicated AI leadership are more likely to see measurable productivity gains.

slants.strategy: If your org has a CAIO, find them. If it doesn't, identify who's filling that gap unofficially — it's often someone in ops or strategy.
slants.operations: AI governance is going to land on someone in your org. Volunteer before it's volunteered for you.
slants.pm: AI program management is becoming its own specialty. If you like coordinating across functions, this is your lane.
slants.admin: Whoever owns AI at your company needs an org-savvy partner. Admins frequently end up in that role.

steps: Search your company's intranet or internal news for 'AI strategy' or 'AI governance.'
Find the name of the person leading it (CAIO, VP of AI, or de facto owner).
Open your company-approved AI tool and ask: "Help me draft a 3-sentence intro email to a [their role] explaining one thing I've learned about AI in my role and one thing I'd like to learn from them."
Save the draft. Don't send it yet — sit with it.
Share whether you found a clear AI leader in your org, or if it's still ambiguous.

homework.title: Use AI to plan a tiny weekend win.
homework.body: It's almost Friday. Pick one small thing you want to feel good about by Sunday night — finishing a book, organizing a drawer, calling a friend, trying a new recipe — and let AI help you plan it.
homework.prompt:
"I want to [the small thing] this weekend. Help me figure out the smallest version that still counts. Ask me 2 questions about what would make it actually happen."

The smallest version that still counts framing kills perfectionism. Try it on bigger things later.

2026-06-12.intro: Friday: the gap between feeling more productive and your org becoming more productive. Three things to think about — and a weekend mindset for tonight.

trends.title: 5× faster individually, 29% ROI organizationally
trends.body: Writer's 2026 enterprise survey put a sharp number on the AI productivity paradox: individual productivity gains of 5×, but only 29% of organizations see significant ROI from generative AI. The gap isn't capability — it's that personal gains aren't compounding into shipped outcomes.

slants.strategy: Don't celebrate individual AI gains as org wins. The work is in the handoffs between people, not the work itself.
slants.operations: Look for places where someone's AI-assisted output bottlenecks on someone else's old process. That's where the leak is.
slants.pm: If devs ship 2× faster but reviews and QA didn't scale, throughput didn't move. Find the new bottleneck.
slants.admin: When AI lets one person do twice as much, the system isn't faster — it just looks faster until it hits the next gate.

steps: Think of one workflow at your job that involves more than one person — a proposal, a sign-off, a content review.
On paper, list the steps and who owns each one.
Next to each step, mark: "This person uses AI here," "They don't," or "Not sure."
Look at the pattern. Where does an AI-fast step hand off to a non-AI step? That's the bottleneck.
Share the bottleneck you spotted in the group — without naming names.

trends.title: Synthesis is the new senior skill
trends.body: As more team members produce AI-drafted work, the new bottleneck becomes synthesizing across multiple AI outputs into a single coherent direction. Anyone can generate; few can synthesize. The synthesis skill is what separates the AI-savvy operator from the AI-flooded one.

slants.strategy: Synthesis across multiple analysts' AI-assisted briefs is the new senior-strategist skill. Practice it deliberately.
slants.operations: When three people send you AI-drafted summaries, your job is to find the contradictions, not to paste them together.
slants.pm: Two AI-generated status updates that say the same thing differently are a warning sign, not redundancy. Look for what's missing.
slants.admin: You see drafts before anyone else. Spot the assumptions repeated across them — that's where the team's blind spot is.

steps: Find two AI-generated outputs you have access to — both yours, or one yours and one from a colleague (with permission, no confidential data).
Open your company-approved AI tool.
Paste both in and add: "What do these two outputs agree on, what do they disagree on, and what does neither one address?"
Read the response carefully. The 'neither one addresses' part is usually the gold.
Share one blind spot you found in the group.

trends.title: The 11.5% productivity bump nobody's bragging about
trends.body: Buried in ManpowerGroup's 2026 data: enterprises reported an average 11.5% increase in net productivity over the past 12 months, partly from AI and partly from broader efficiency work. Real, modest, hard-won. Not the 10× headlines — but compounding year over year, this is what changes how companies actually run.

slants.strategy: Plan for compounding, not breakthroughs. 11% per year is over 50% in five — and it's exactly where good operators add value.
slants.operations: Steady process improvements are back in fashion. Pair each AI experiment with a measurable old-school KPI.
slants.pm: Velocity charts should track sustained gains, not spikes. A 10% rolling improvement is the win.
slants.admin: Small efficiencies you find — better templates, smarter folders, AI-assisted scheduling — add up faster than they look.

steps: Write down three things you do this week that take noticeably less time than they did a year ago.
For each, note what changed (tool, habit, skill, team).
Pick the one with the biggest gain.
Open your AI tool and ask: "Here's something I do faster now: [describe]. What's the next improvement I could make to compound the gain?"
Share your top time-saver — and the next improvement AI suggested — in the group.

homework.title: End-of-week mindset reset.
homework.body: Fridays are good for closing tabs — literal and mental. Tonight, use AI to help you actually finish your week.
homework.prompt:
"I'm trying to end this week feeling like I closed it well. Ask me 3 questions to help me decide what's worth carrying into next week and what I should let go of."

The trick is making AI a reflection prompt, not an answer machine. The 'let go' question is the one that matters.

2026-06-13.intro: Saturday's drop is lighter on purpose. Three angles on how AI can teach you something this weekend — no work allowed.

trends.title: AI tutors got weirdly good in 2026
trends.body: One of AI's most underrated uses this year — quietly bigger than enterprise pilots in many people's daily lives — is personalized tutoring. Patient, infinite, judgment-free explainers for whatever you've been meaning to learn. The barrier was never finding an explanation; it was finding one that matched your level.

slants.strategy: Learning to learn faster is a leadership skill. AI tutoring is the cheapest way to compound it.
slants.operations: Pick one process at your company you don't fully understand. Ask AI to explain it from scratch.
slants.pm: Quietly studying a domain through AI before a project lands is the new prep work. Free and invisible.
slants.admin: If you've ever felt 'I should know this by now,' AI is the no-judgment teacher you deserved years ago.

steps: Pick one thing you've quietly been meaning to understand — a finance concept, a piece of tech, a historical event, anything.
Open a free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity).
Use this prompt: "Teach me [topic] like I'm a curious adult with no background. Use a metaphor I'd recognize from everyday life. Then ask me one question to check if I got it."
Answer the check-in question honestly.
Share what you learned in the group — and whether the metaphor actually helped.

trends.title: Reading AI critically is the real skill
trends.body: Plenty of people can ask AI questions. The smaller, more valuable group can spot when the answer is wrong. That muscle isn't built by using AI more — it's built by deliberately challenging it.

slants.strategy: Skeptical readers of AI output make better strategic decisions. Train the muscle in low-stakes settings.
slants.operations: The first answer is rarely the best one. Make 'ask twice with different framing' a habit.
slants.pm: Verification beats generation. The job is increasingly to check what AI claims, not to ask it more things.
slants.admin: You already double-check exec emails before they go. Same instinct, new collaborator.

steps: Pick a topic you actually know well — a hobby, a former job, a specialty.
Open AI and ask: "Give me 5 facts about [your area of expertise]."
Read carefully. Find at least one fact that's wrong, misleading, or oversimplified.
Reply: "Fact #X is wrong because [your correction]. What else might you have gotten wrong?"
Share in the group what AI got wrong about something you know.

trends.title: Hobby AI is the easiest practice ground
trends.body: There's a pattern across communities like this one: members who use AI on something they genuinely love — a recipe, a fantasy league, a garden, a creative project — build prompting skill faster than members who only use it at work. The reason: they don't tolerate bad answers when it's something they care about.

slants.strategy: Your weekend AI practice sharpens your weekday judgment. Don't treat them as separate.
slants.operations: The instincts you develop fighting AI on a personal project transfer directly to fighting it on a work problem.
slants.pm: Project-managing a fun side project with AI teaches scope creep faster than any pro training.
slants.admin: Using AI on something personal builds the taste that work AI doesn't earn from you.

steps: Pick one hobby, interest, or personal project you'd love to spend an hour on this weekend.
Open a free AI tool.
Ask: "Help me get one hour of meaningful progress on [thing] this weekend. Ask me 3 questions first, then give me a 3-step plan."
Do at least one of the steps.
Share what you actually did in the group — even if it's tiny.

homework.title: Teach AI something this weekend.
homework.body: Flip the script. AI usually teaches you. Tonight, teach it. The act of explaining clarifies your own thinking — that's the real win.
homework.prompt:
"I want to teach you something I know well: [topic]. I'll explain it in my own words. After I do, ask me 2 questions that would help you understand it better."

The 'ask me questions to deepen your understanding' framing turns AI into a curious student. Surprisingly clarifying.

2026-06-14.intro: Three things worth thinking about today — and one for you to try at home.

trends.title: The strategic memo is being rebuilt — fast
trends.body: Microsoft's 2026 Work Trend Index just landed. 49% of Copilot conversations now support cognitive work — analysis, problem-solving, strategic thinking — and 58% of AI users say they're producing work they couldn't have done a year ago. The work isn't disappearing. It's being promoted.

slants.strategy: You're shifting from writer to editor + pressure-tester.
slants.operations: Synthesis across systems is now a 30-min job, not a 3-day one.
slants.pm: Status reports get auto-drafted; framing the right questions is the new value-add.
slants.admin: Your judgment on tone, context, and 'what the exec actually meant' becomes the premium skill.

steps: Pick a document you wrote last quarter — a recommendation, memo, or proposal. Make sure it doesn't contain confidential client or customer information.
Open your company-approved AI tool (Copilot, Gemini Enterprise, Claude for Work — whatever's blessed at your company).
Paste the document in, then add this prompt: "Argue against this from the perspective of a skeptical executive. Be specific about the weakest assumptions."
Read the response carefully and identify one point you hadn't considered before.
Share that insight in the group — what surprised you most?

trends.title: The Transformation Paradox
trends.body: Deloitte's 2026 enterprise report puts it plainly: companies are layering AI onto legacy processes instead of redesigning work holistically. The investment isn't compounding because the workflow underneath is still pre-AI.

slants.strategy: The QBR was built for a pre-AI world. Rethink the inputs and outputs.
slants.operations: Your SOPs probably encode 2019 assumptions.
slants.pm: "Weekly check-ins" exist because info moved slowly. Does it still?
slants.admin: Calendar-and-email triage workflows are ripe for a clean-slate redesign.

steps: Pick ONE recurring meeting or process you own — the QBR, a weekly check-in, a status report.
Open a notes app or grab a piece of paper.
Write at the top: "If AI were a teammate from minute one, this would [...]"
Finish the sentence. Don't overthink it — the first instinct is usually the most honest one.
Share your one-sentence answer in the group.

trends.title: AI fluency is the new literacy
trends.body: Stanford HAI's 2026 AI Index: AI-related skills now appear in 2.5% of all U.S. job postings — a 297% increase over a decade. This isn't "learn to code." It's learn to direct, evaluate, and trust-but-verify AI output.

slants.strategy: Prompting is problem-framing. The clearer your ask, the sharper your strategy.
slants.operations: Knowing when AI is wrong saves more time than AI being right.
slants.pm: Agent orchestration is the next PM specialty.
slants.admin: You already know how things actually work here. AI doesn't.

steps: Open a blank document or note.
List your top 3 weekly recurring tasks — the things you do every week without fail.
Next to each task, write one of three labels: "AI could draft this", "AI could review my work on this", or "Only I can do this."
Look at your split. Pick one task you didn't realize AI could help with.
Share your split in the group, and call out that one surprise task.

homework.title: Brand new to AI? Start with something personal tonight.
homework.body: The fastest way to lose the fear: use AI on something where there's no security policy and no judgment. Try one of these in your free tool of choice (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity all have free web versions):
homework.prompt:
"I'm trying to [plan a weekend / write a tough message / figure out a recipe from what's in my fridge / understand a confusing bill]. Ask me 3 questions before you help, so your answer actually fits."

That last line — ask me questions first — is the single most useful prompting habit you'll ever learn.

2026-06-16.intro: Tuesday: what separates the orgs winning with AI from the ones still pitching it. Plus something small for you to try tonight.

trends.title: The 25% gap
trends.body: Hackett Group's 2026 Enterprise Key Issues Study found that the organizations getting transformational AI results — 25%+ improvements across customer experience, employee experience, and productivity — aren't the ones deploying the most tools. They're the ones reimagining their operating model. The lever is structural, not technological.

slants.strategy: If your AI roadmap is a tool list, you're losing to orgs whose AI roadmap is an operating-model redesign.
slants.operations: The most underrated AI question right now: "Which of our standard processes deserve to exist at all?"
slants.pm: Project portfolios are still designed around human cadence. The next wave reshapes the portfolio itself, not just the projects in it.
slants.admin: If you've been told to 'make existing workflows faster,' push back. The question is whether the workflow should look the same at all.

steps: Pick a single workflow you own end-to-end (intake to delivery).
On paper or in a doc, draw it as 5–7 steps from start to finish.
For each step, mark one of three labels: "Required for value," "Habit, not value," or "Worth questioning."
Look at the 'Habit' and 'Worth questioning' labels — that's your AI-redesign opportunity list.
Share your most surprising 'Habit, not value' step in the group.

trends.title: The 'strategy theater' gap
trends.body: Writer's 2026 enterprise survey landed a hard finding: 75% of executives admit their company's AI strategy is "more for show" than actual guidance, with 39% lacking any formal revenue plan tied to AI. There's a vacuum at the strategy layer — and operators who can connect strategy to measurable outcomes just became more valuable.

slants.strategy: If you're in strategy and your AI deck is real, you're rarer than you think. Lean into outcome metrics, not capability lists.
slants.operations: When leadership's strategy is unclear, ops becomes the de facto translator. Document the gap, then propose the bridge.
slants.pm: 'Connect strategy to delivery' has always been a PM superpower. AI just made it 10× more visible.
slants.admin: If you can summarize what's actually being asked (vs. what's being announced), that clarity is a leadership skill in disguise.

steps: Find your company's most recent AI announcement or strategy doc that's safe to share with an AI tool.
Open your company-approved AI tool.
Paste in the doc and add this prompt: "Pull out every specific, measurable outcome promised here. If there are none, say so."
Look at the list. Notice the ratio of concrete outcomes to aspirational language.
Share one observation in the group — was the strategy specific, or mostly directional?

trends.title: Knowing which AI mode to use is the new skill
trends.body: Microsoft's 2026 Copilot update introduced "Quick Response" and "Think Deeper" modes — two different reasoning depths for two different kinds of questions. The skill isn't using the tool; it's knowing which mode for which question. Expect every major AI tool to follow this pattern.

slants.strategy: Quick Response for status, Think Deeper for trade-off decisions. Mixing them up wastes either time or quality.
slants.operations: Most ops questions are quick lookups; a few are real analysis. Match the mode to the task and your team's output improves.
slants.pm: Use Quick Response for stand-up summaries. Use Think Deeper for risk assessment and dependency mapping.
slants.admin: Drafting an email? Quick. Resolving a calendar conflict that affects three execs? Deeper. Trust the difference.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool that offers different reasoning modes (Copilot, Gemini, Claude, ChatGPT all have variations).
Pick the SAME work question and ask it twice — once in fast mode, once in slow/deep mode.
Compare the answers. Was the slower one actually better, or just longer?
Decide your own rule of thumb: "For [type of question], I'll use [mode]."
Share your rule of thumb in the group — we'll learn from each other's defaults.

homework.title: Use AI to win an argument with yourself tonight.
homework.body: Most of us have one small decision we've been putting off — a purchase, a route change, a routine swap. Tonight, let AI play both sides.
homework.prompt:
"I'm trying to decide whether to [the thing]. Steelman the case for doing it. Then steelman the case against. Don't give me a recommendation — I'll decide."

The trick is asking for both sides, no recommendation. You'll think more clearly than if you'd asked for advice.

2026-06-17.intro: Wednesday: the gap between having AI and winning with AI keeps widening. Three things to think about — and a low-key experiment for tonight.

trends.title: 74% of AI's value goes to 20% of companies
trends.body: PwC's 2026 AI Performance Study surveyed 1,217 senior executives across 25 sectors and found a stark divide: 20% of organizations capture nearly three-quarters of AI's total economic value. The differentiator isn't budget or tooling — it's direction. Leaders aimed AI at growth and new revenue, not headcount cuts, and earned 7.2× the financial gains of their peers.

slants.strategy: If your AI business case leads with cost savings, you're optimizing for the smaller prize. Reframe around revenue.
slants.operations: The ops teams winning aren't automating old tasks faster — they're building workflows that didn't exist before.
slants.pm: When leadership asks for the AI ROI story, push for growth metrics alongside efficiency ones. The data backs you up.
slants.admin: If your org is using AI mostly to cut costs, the gains will plateau. The real value is in what AI lets you do that you couldn't before.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.
Think of one process your team currently uses AI to speed up — drafting, summarizing, formatting, anything.
Paste this prompt: "We currently use AI to do [task] faster. What's a completely new capability or output AI could enable for this team — something we're not doing at all today?"
Read the suggestions. Pick the one that feels most realistic.
Share your best 'new capability' idea in the group — growth ideas beat efficiency ideas.

trends.title: 60% of executives use AI to decide — 5% manage it well
trends.body: Deloitte's 2026 Global Human Capital Trends survey of 9,000+ leaders across 89 countries found something uncomfortable: 60% of executives now regularly use AI to support decisions, but only 5% say they manage that process well. Only 6% report meaningful progress redesigning work for human-AI collaboration. The tool arrived; the governance didn't.

slants.strategy: Your leadership is almost certainly using AI for decisions already. The question is whether anyone's defined when to trust it and when not to.
slants.operations: If AI is shaping decisions upstream but your processes don't account for that, your inputs changed without your knowledge.
slants.pm: Decision logs just got more important. If AI influenced a go/no-go call, that should be traceable.
slants.admin: When an executive's thinking was shaped by AI, the context you provide — what AI didn't see — becomes more critical, not less.

steps: Think of one decision you or your team made recently where AI played any role — even just generating options or summarizing data.
Write down: what did AI contribute, and what did a human add on top?

Open your company-approved AI tool and paste: "Here's how we made a recent decision: [describe it briefly, no confidential details]. What's the biggest risk of relying on AI at the step where we used it? What safeguard would you add?"
Decide whether the suggested safeguard is worth adopting.
Share one decision-making safeguard you'd recommend in the group.

trends.title: The proficiency multiplier
trends.body: Gartner's 1Q26 Global Labor Market Survey of 12,004 employees across 40 countries found that AI-proficient workers are 2× as productive, 2.3× more likely to deliver high-quality work, and 3.2× more likely to drive process improvements. But only 17% of employees qualify as "AI Champions" — while 56% remain resisters. The gap isn't access. It's practice.

slants.strategy: Proficiency compounds. A 2× productivity edge today becomes a career-defining gap in two years. Invest the reps now.
slants.operations: The 3.2× process improvement number is the one that matters most for ops. The people fixing workflows are the ones using AI to find what's broken.
slants.pm: If your team has a few AI Champions and mostly resisters, velocity is capped by the resisters. Make proficiency a team goal, not an individual one.
slants.admin: You don't need to be a power user overnight. Moving from 'never' to 'sometimes' is where the biggest quality jump happens.

steps: Honestly assess yourself: are you an AI Champion (use it regularly, feel positive), an occasional user, or a resister?
Pick one task you did today without AI — an email, a summary, a plan.
Redo it with your company-approved AI tool. Time both versions.
Note not just speed but quality: did AI catch something you missed, or did you catch something AI got wrong?
Share your self-assessment and one observation from the comparison in the group.

homework.title: Tonight, let AI help you explain something you already know.
homework.body: The fastest way to build AI confidence: use it on a topic where you're the expert. You'll learn to direct, correct, and refine — with zero stakes. Open any free tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity):
homework.prompt:
"I know a lot about [something personal you're genuinely good at — cooking, a sport, a craft, a subject]. Help me explain it to someone who's brand new. Ask me 3 questions about my audience first, then draft a short explanation I can review and edit."

The ask questions first, then draft pattern works everywhere. Tonight you'll see why editing AI is more useful than writing from scratch.

2026-06-18.intro: Thursday: AI budgets are getting real, and governance gaps are showing up fast. Three things worth your attention — and something low-stakes for tonight.

trends.title: The real AI bottleneck isn't the model — it's the data
trends.body: Gartner surveyed 353 data and AI leaders and found that organizations with successful AI initiatives invest up to 4× more in data quality, governance, and change management than those with poor outcomes. Only 39% of tech leaders are confident their current AI investments will pay off financially. And 60% of AI projects are expected to be abandoned this year — not because the AI failed, but because the data wasn't ready.

slants.strategy: If your AI roadmap doesn't have a data quality line item, it's a wish list. The 4× gap is real.
slants.operations: Before you pilot another AI tool, audit the data feeding it. Clean inputs beat clever models every time.
slants.pm: Add 'data readiness' as a gate in your AI project plan. It's the step most teams skip — and most failures trace back to.
slants.admin: When someone says 'the AI isn't working,' the first question is often about the data, not the tool. You're closer to that data than you think.

steps: Pick one report or dashboard your team relies on for decisions.

Open your company-approved AI tool and paste this prompt: "I rely on a [type of report] for [what decisions]. What are the most common data quality problems that would make AI unreliable for this kind of work? Give me a 5-point checklist I can use to audit it."
Run through the checklist against your actual report. Note which items pass and which don't.
Share one data quality gap you discovered in the group — and whether it surprised you.

trends.title: 25% of AI budgets just got pushed to next year
trends.body: Forrester's 2026 technology predictions flagged a hard correction: enterprises will defer 25% of planned AI spend into 2027 as financial rigor catches up to vendor promises. Only 15% of AI decision-makers reported an EBITDA lift in the past 12 months, and fewer than one-third can tie AI's value to their organization's financial growth. The CFO is now the gatekeeper.

slants.strategy: The AI business case just shifted from 'possibility' to 'proof.' If you can show measurable value on a small project, you'll survive the budget cut.
slants.operations: Budget tightening means fewer new tools and more pressure to prove the ones you have. Document your wins now — you'll need them.
slants.pm: Expect AI project portfolios to shrink. The surviving projects will be the ones with clear ROI timelines, not the flashiest demos.
slants.admin: If AI tools at your company feel like they're stalling, it's not just you. Budgets are tightening, and proven use cases get priority.

steps: Think of one AI tool or feature your team currently uses (or has been piloting).
Write down, in one sentence, the specific value it delivers. If you can't, that's the finding.

Open your company-approved AI tool and paste: "Help me write a 3-sentence business case for continuing to use [tool/feature] based on this value: [your sentence]. Make it specific enough for a finance review."
Read the draft. Could it survive a CFO asking 'so what?'
Share your one-sentence value statement in the group — brevity is the test.

trends.title: The binary governance trap is killing AI agents
trends.body: Gartner's May 2026 research warned that 40% of enterprises will demote or decommission autonomous AI agents by 2027 because of governance gaps discovered only after production incidents. The root cause: organizations are treating agent governance as binary — either fully locked down or fully trusted. Over-restricting simple agents slows delivery; under-restricting autonomous ones creates real risk.

slants.strategy: Agent governance is a strategy decision, not an IT checkbox. Define tiers of autonomy before your first production incident does it for you.
slants.operations: The ops team that builds a clear escalation path — 'agent handles X, human reviews Y' — becomes the template everyone copies.
slants.pm: Scope every AI agent like a team member: clear authority, clear boundaries, clear escalation. Ambiguity is where incidents start.
slants.admin: If an AI agent can send on your behalf or access shared systems, someone should have defined exactly what it's allowed to do. If they haven't, flag it.

steps: List any AI tools or agents at your workplace that can take actions — scheduling, sending, approving, editing — not just answer questions.
For each one, write down: "What can it do without asking a human first?"
If the answer is vague or you're not sure, that's the governance gap Gartner is warning about.
Open your AI tool and ask: "What's a simple 3-tier framework for governing AI agents based on their level of autonomy? Make it practical for a non-technical team."
Share the most useful tier distinction you found in the group.

homework.title: Tonight, let AI help you prepare for an awkward conversation.
homework.body: Everyone's got one — a message to send, a boundary to set, a favor to ask, a topic to raise. No work context needed. Open a free tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity):
homework.prompt:
"I need to [describe the conversation — ask a neighbor something, bring up a topic with a friend, negotiate with a contractor, talk to a family member about X]. Give me 3 different ways to open the conversation, ranked from most direct to most gentle. For each, tell me what tone it sets."

The ranked with tone labels trick helps you choose consciously instead of defaulting to whatever feels safest.

2026-06-19.intro: Friday: AI is reshaping org charts, stretching the workday, and putting a premium on thinking without it. Three things to sit with — and one to try tonight.

trends.title: The org chart is flattening — and your role in it is shifting
trends.body: Gartner's top predictions for 2026 include a structural one: 20% of organizations will use AI to flatten their org structure, eliminating more than half of current middle management positions. The tasks being absorbed aren't the leadership ones — they're the scheduling, status reporting, and performance tracking that filled the administrative half of managing. What remains is the part that was always harder to define: judgment, mentorship, and navigating ambiguity.

slants.strategy: If your value is synthesizing information upward, AI already does that. Your edge is interpreting what the data doesn't say.
slants.operations: Fewer layers means decisions reach you faster — and expect a response faster. Build the muscle for quicker, more autonomous calls.
slants.pm: The reporting-up function of a PM is getting compressed. The value shifts to cross-functional coordination AI can't orchestrate.
slants.admin: When layers disappear, institutional memory gets thinner. You're carrying more of it than the org chart suggests.

steps: List the recurring tasks you do each week that exist primarily to keep someone else informed — status updates, check-in prep, progress summaries.
For each one, ask yourself: "Could an AI draft this from data that already exists, with me just reviewing it?"

Open your company-approved AI tool and paste: "I spend time each week on these info-relay tasks: [list yours]. For each one, suggest how AI could draft or automate it, and what human judgment I'd still need to add."
Identify the task where AI could save you the most time — and the one where your judgment matters most.
Share both in the group: what can you hand off, and what's irreplaceably yours?

trends.title: The infinite workday is real — and AI isn't fixing it yet
trends.body: Microsoft's latest Work Trend Index report puts numbers on what most people already feel: workers are interrupted up to 275 times a day, receive 117 emails and 153 chat messages daily, and meetings after 8 p.m. have risen 16% year over year. The workday didn't shrink when AI arrived — it stretched. The risk, Microsoft warns, is using AI to accelerate a broken system instead of redesigning the rhythm of work.

slants.strategy: If your team gained productivity but not capacity, the system absorbed the gains. That's a strategy problem, not a tools problem.
slants.operations: 275 daily interruptions means your team's deep work is fragmented into two-minute slices. Protecting focus time is now an ops priority.
slants.pm: 60% of meetings are ad hoc. Before layering AI onto the calendar, audit whether half those meetings need to exist at all.
slants.admin: You're on the front lines of the infinite workday. If AI helps you batch and triage faster, use the saved time to protect your boundaries — not fill them.

steps: Open your calendar and count how many meetings you had this week. Estimate how many were truly necessary versus 'could have been an async update.'

Pick one recurring meeting that feels low-value. Open your company-approved AI tool and paste: "This meeting happens [frequency] and covers [topics]. Suggest a simple async alternative — a structured update format that could replace the live meeting for a team of [size]."
Review the suggestion. Could your team realistically try it for two weeks?
Share your 'meeting I'd replace' and the async alternative in the group.

trends.title: Independent thinking is becoming a testable skill
trends.body: Gartner's strategic predictions for 2026 include a sharp warning: critical-thinking atrophy from GenAI use will push 50% of organizations to require "AI-free" skills assessments. The concern isn't that people use AI — it's that over-reliance erodes the reasoning muscles underneath. In hiring and evaluation, high-stakes industries are already building methods designed to isolate human judgment from AI-assisted output.

slants.strategy: The strategist who can think clearly without AI and think better with it has a compounding advantage. Both muscles matter.
slants.operations: When systems fail and AI tools go down with them, the team that can reason through the problem manually is the one that recovers first.
slants.pm: If your team can't estimate, prioritize, or assess risk without AI, that's a single point of failure in your delivery model.
slants.admin: Your ability to spot what 'feels off' in a document or process — before AI confirms it — is exactly the kind of thinking these assessments protect.

steps: Pick a decision or analysis you recently used AI to help with — a recommendation, a comparison, a summary.
Set a 10-minute timer. Without opening any AI tool, redo the core reasoning on paper or in a blank doc. What's your independent conclusion?
Compare your solo version with the AI-assisted one. Where did they agree? Where did they differ?
Open your AI tool and ask: "What are the most common ways people over-rely on AI for [type of task you just tested]? Give me 3 warning signs and 3 habits to stay sharp."
Share your biggest takeaway in the group — did your independent reasoning hold up?

homework.title: Tonight, let AI help you start something you've been putting off.
homework.body: We spend all week using AI for work problems. Tonight, point it at something personal you've been meaning to do — a weekend trip, a home project, learning something new, organizing a get-together. Open a free tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity):
homework.prompt:
"I've been meaning to [describe the thing you keep putting off] but I never get started because [your honest reason]. Break it into 3 small steps I could begin tonight, and make the first step so easy I can't say no."

The make the first step so easy I can't say no trick is a real productivity technique — AI just makes it personal and specific.

2026-06-20.intro: Saturday: the week's patterns are coming into focus. Three things to carry forward — and something for just you tonight.

trends.title: Your org's complexity is the thing blocking AI
trends.body: McKinsey's State of Organizations 2026 surveyed more than 10,000 senior leaders across 15 countries. The finding that should concern strategists most: 86% of leaders say their organization is not prepared to integrate AI into day-to-day operations. The culprit isn't the technology — two-thirds of leaders say their orgs are overly complex and inefficient. Traditional fixes like restructuring and cost cuts are delivering diminishing returns. McKinsey's prescription: stop redesigning the org chart and start redesigning how work flows.

slants.strategy: If AI keeps stalling, look at how many handoffs, approvals, and meetings stand between an idea and a decision. That's your bottleneck.
slants.operations: Every unnecessary handoff is a point where AI can't help — because the process, not the tool, is the problem.
slants.pm: Before automating a workflow, map it. If it takes more than seven steps to go from input to output, simplify first, then automate.
slants.admin: You see the bottlenecks daily — the approvals that sit for a week, the cc chains that slow everything down. That visibility is valuable.

steps: Pick one workflow you're involved in that touches 3 or more people or teams before it's complete.
Sketch the steps from start to finish — who does what, in what order. Paper, whiteboard, or doc — whatever's fastest.

Open your company-approved AI tool and paste: "Here's a workflow that involves [X] steps and [Y] handoffs: [describe it briefly, no confidential details]. Identify the 2 steps that add the most delay without adding the most value."
Compare the AI's answer with your gut feeling. Where do things actually slow down?
Share your biggest 'delay without value' step in the group.

trends.title: Fear is the real AI adoption barrier — and it's self-reinforcing
trends.body: Forrester's 2026 workforce research found that 43% of employees fear widespread job loss from AI, and a quarter worry about their own role — even though actual AI-driven layoffs have been modest. The catch: that fear keeps people from learning AI, which keeps them unprepared, which makes the fear feel more justified. Only 51% of organizations train non-technical staff on AI, and just 16% of workers score high on Forrester's AI readiness index.

slants.strategy: Fear is a strategy problem. If your people won't adopt the tools, your AI roadmap is a plan nobody executes.
slants.operations: The ops team that creates safe, low-stakes AI practice space does more for adoption than any mandate from leadership.
slants.pm: If a team member resists an AI tool, the blocker is probably anxiety, not capability. Address the feeling before the feature.
slants.admin: If you're nervous about AI, you're in the statistical majority. The antidote isn't pressure — it's one small experiment that goes well.

steps: Rate your own AI comfort level honestly: 1 (avoid it) to 5 (use it daily, feel confident).
If you're below a 4, name the specific thing that makes you hesitant — not sure how to start, worried about mistakes, concern about job impact, or something else.

Open a free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity) and ask: "I'm new to using AI at work and my biggest hesitation is [your honest reason]. Give me one safe, 5-minute experiment I could try this weekend to build confidence — nothing that touches work data."
Try the experiment. Notice how it feels.
Share your comfort rating and what you tried in the group — honesty helps everyone.

trends.title: The AI layoff boomerang
trends.body: Gartner predicted in February 2026 that by 2027, half of the companies that cut staff due to AI will rehire for similar functions — just under different titles. A survey of 321 customer service leaders found only 20% actually reduced headcount because of AI, and 55% of employers who made early cuts already regret them. AI is changing roles, not eliminating them at the scale the headlines suggest.

slants.strategy: If your workforce plan assumes AI replaces headcount, stress-test it. The data says most orgs underestimate how much human judgment they still need.
slants.operations: Teams that cut too fast are scrambling to rebuild institutional knowledge. Retaining and upskilling costs less than rehiring.
slants.pm: When leadership proposes AI-driven headcount reductions on your project, bring this data. The boomerang is predictable and expensive.
slants.admin: If AI anxiety is in the air at your workplace, share this: half the companies that cut will be hiring again within a year.

steps: Find one recent headline about AI replacing jobs — you've probably seen several this week.

Open a free AI tool and paste the headline with this prompt: "Analyze this headline about AI and job loss. What does the actual data say versus what the headline implies? What's the most important nuance the headline leaves out?"
Read the analysis. Notice the gap between the headline and the full picture.
Now ask a follow-up: "Given this nuance, what's one concrete thing a [your role] should focus on to stay valuable — not in theory, but this quarter?"
Share the headline and the most useful nuance you found in the group.

homework.title: This weekend, let AI help you learn something you've always been curious about.
homework.body: No work, no stakes, no performance review. Just curiosity. Pick a topic you've always wanted to understand — how the stock market works, why bread rises, what your car's dashboard lights mean, how to negotiate a lease. Open a free tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity):
homework.prompt:
"I've always wanted to understand [your topic] but never had the right entry point. Explain it to me like I'm smart but have zero background. Start with the one thing I need to understand first, then build from there. Keep it under 300 words."

The explain like I'm smart but have zero background framing gets you clear answers without the condescension. Try it on anything.

2026-06-21.intro: Sunday check-in. The AI adoption numbers are in, and the gap between organizations that have AI and those using it well keeps growing — and the cause isn't the technology.

trends.title: The 'enablement illusion' is the new AI risk
trends.body: Most leaders are measuring AI adoption by access, not by whether it changes anything. Gartner's May 2026 global survey of 12,000+ employees found that workers who are AI-proficient across multiple use cases are 3.2 times more likely to drive effective process improvements than those with basic access. By 2027, Gartner predicts half of enterprises without a people-first AI strategy will lose their best AI talent.

slants.strategy: Reframe your AI KPIs: shift from 'licenses deployed' to 'workflows meaningfully changed' — that's where the ROI signal actually lives.
slants.operations: Audit which teams have AI access but no structured time or training to use it — that's where the enablement illusion costs the most.
slants.pm: Build AI proficiency as a project success criterion, not just adoption rate — capability depth drives results, not tool access alone.
slants.admin: If you've been given AI tools but no clear guidance on using them, that's your organization's gap to close, not yours — advocate for a structured use-case session with your team.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.
Think of one recurring task you do weekly that feels repetitive — a status update, a summary, or a data lookup.
Paste this prompt: I need to [describe your task]. Walk me through how AI could help me do this faster, and give me a sample prompt I can use next time.
Try the sample prompt on a real, non-sensitive example.
Note what worked and what you would adjust.
Share your task and the sample prompt in the community group — help others see what's possible.

trends.title: Agents are in production, but half of companies skipped the redesign
trends.body: Active AI agents in the Microsoft 365 ecosystem grew 15x year over year, per Microsoft's 2026 Work Trend Index of 20,000 knowledge workers. Deloitte found that nearly half of organizations introduced AI without redesigning the workflows or roles around it — so agents are running on top of broken processes instead of better ones.

slants.strategy: Before scaling agentic AI, map one existing workflow end-to-end and identify what's being automated versus what's just being accelerated — that difference determines your actual efficiency gain.
slants.operations: If your team uses agents to speed up a manual handoff, the handoff is the problem — use this as a trigger to redesign the process, not just automate it.
slants.pm: Add a workflow redesign checkpoint to your AI rollout process — deploying an agent without it is like adding a faster engine to a car with no steering.
slants.admin: If a new AI tool was added to your workflow without explanation, it's fair to ask how it changes what you're supposed to do — that question is worth raising.

steps: Pick one process your team uses regularly that has more than three steps.
Open your company-approved AI tool and type: Here is a workflow my team uses: [describe it in plain English]. Which steps could an AI agent handle, and which require human judgment?
Review the response — does it flag any steps you hadn't considered?
Write down one step that could go to AI and one that definitely shouldn't.
Share your findings in the community group — what was the most surprising step to keep human?

trends.title: How managers model AI use shapes outcomes more than tools do
trends.body: Microsoft's 2026 Work Trend Index found that when managers actively model their own AI use, employees report a 30-point lift in trust in agentic AI and a 22-point lift in how critically they evaluate AI output. Organizational culture accounts for more than twice the impact on AI value as individual employee mindset — 67% versus 32%.

slants.strategy: If AI adoption is stalling in your org, look at your managers before your tech stack — culture and manager behavior are 2x more predictive of AI ROI than individual mindset.
slants.operations: Have team leads share one AI-assisted output per week in team meetings, with a note on what worked and what needed fixing — that's the modeling that moves metrics.
slants.pm: Psychological safety around AI experimentation is measurable: teams where managers encourage trying and failing with AI tools report up to 20 points higher AI readiness — build that into your team norms.
slants.admin: When your manager shares how they use AI, pay attention — it signals what the organization considers safe to try, and it's worth taking note of.

steps: Think about the last time you shared work that AI helped you with — did you mention the AI, or leave it invisible?

Open your company-approved AI tool and use this prompt: Write a casual 3-sentence note I could share with a coworker explaining one way I used AI this week. Keep it conversational and honest about what it did well.
Edit the draft to sound like you.
Share it in the community group — modeling starts with one person going first.

homework.title: Plan a trip you've been putting off
homework.body: Got a summer trip living in your head as 'someday'? Turn it into an actual plan — no work data, no risk. Try this in ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini (all free):
homework.prompt:
I want to plan a trip to [your destination or 'somewhere new within a few hours of me']. Before you give me suggestions, ask me 3 questions that will help you understand my travel style and budget.

After I answer, give me a 3-day itinerary with one 'splurge' option and one 'budget-friendly' version for each day.

2026-06-22.intro: Monday. Most companies have AI agents in production now — so this week's real question isn't whether to deploy, it's whether anyone's actually steering them. Three things to think about, and a five-minute experiment to start the week.

trends.title: Most AI agents are running without a kill switch
trends.body: Writer's 2026 AI Adoption in the Enterprise survey (with Workplace Intelligence, covering 1,200 executives and 1,200 employees) found that 36% of organizations have no formal plan for supervising AI agents, and 35% admit they couldn't immediately 'pull the plug' on one that went rogue. Meanwhile 67% of executives believe their company has already suffered a data leak from unapproved AI tools, and 55% describe AI use at their company as a 'chaotic free-for-all.'

slants.strategy: Governance is now a competitive advantage, not a compliance tax — the orgs that can safely say 'yes' to AI will out-execute the ones still saying 'wait.' Make agent oversight a metric leadership actually reviews.
slants.operations: For every agent your team runs, write down two things this week: who owns it, and how you'd stop it. If you can't answer either, that's your highest-priority fix.
slants.pm: Add a 'named owner and documented off-switch' line to your AI deployment checklist — an agent without both isn't production-ready, no matter how well it demos.
slants.admin: If you're using an AI tool that was never officially approved, you're not alone — but flag it so it can be made safe rather than hidden. Shadow AI only becomes a breach when nobody knows it's there.

steps: List every AI tool or agent you personally use for work — including the unofficial ones.

Open your company-approved AI tool and paste: Here is a list of AI tools I use for work: [your list]. For each one, ask me a question that would help determine whether it's touching sensitive company data.
Answer the questions honestly for yourself.
Flag anything that touches sensitive data to your manager or IT — frame it as 'help me use this safely,' not a confession.
Share one thing you learned about your own AI footprint in the community group.

trends.title: 97% deployed agents. Only 29% are seeing returns.
trends.body: The same 2026 enterprise research found that while 97% of organizations deployed AI agents in the past year — and 80%+ of the Fortune 500 now run them in production — only 29% report significant ROI, and 79% say they're facing real challenges scaling AI, a double-digit jump from 2025. The bottleneck isn't access to agents; it's the workflows and oversight around them.

slants.strategy: If your ROI story is still 'we deployed X agents,' you're measuring activity, not value. Trace one agent to a dollar saved or an hour freed — if you can't, that's the gap to close before deploying the next one.
slants.operations: Resist adding another agent this week. Take one you already have and remove a step of friction around it — most agent ROI leaks out through the human handoffs at the edges.
slants.pm: Treat every agent rollout like a product with a success metric defined before launch. 'It's live' is a milestone, not an outcome.
slants.admin: If an agent is supposed to save you time but you're spending more time double-checking it, that's real signal — surface it. The fix is usually upstream, not your patience.

steps: Pick one AI agent or automation your team already uses.

Open your company-approved AI tool and type: An AI agent at my company does [describe what it does]. Help me figure out whether it's actually saving time — ask me 3 questions about how the work flowed before and after.
Answer the questions and notice where the real time goes now.
Identify one handoff or check around the agent that could be smoothed.
Share the friction point you found in the community group — odds are someone else has it too.

trends.title: The people going deep with AI are pulling away
trends.body: Across 2026 workforce research, the divide keeps widening between casual users and 'super-users' who weave AI through multiple tasks: super-users report saving roughly nine hours a week and are several times more productive than slow adopters. Yet 56% of the global workforce says they've had no recent AI training, and 57% lack access to mentorship. The capability is available; the structured chance to build it often isn't.

slants.strategy: Your biggest AI ROI lever this quarter may be training, not tooling — the proficiency gap, not the access gap, is what separates your top performers from the rest.
slants.operations: Block 30 minutes this week for your team to swap one AI workflow each. Peer learning closes the proficiency gap faster than any vendor webinar.
slants.pm: Build skill-building into project timelines, not as an afterthought — a team that's nine hours a week more efficient pays back the training time inside the first sprint.
slants.admin: You don't need permission to get better at this. Pick one task you do often and spend 15 minutes finding a sharper way to do it with AI — that compounds.

steps: Think of one task you did with AI this week that felt just 'okay.'

Open your company-approved AI tool and paste: Last time I used AI for [task], I [describe what you did]. Suggest three ways I could have gotten a better result, and rewrite my approach as a reusable prompt.
Save the reusable prompt somewhere you'll find it again.
Use it on your next real task.
Share your reusable prompt in the community group — give someone else a nine-hours-a-week head start.

homework.title: Design your ideal Saturday
homework.body: Mondays are easier when there's something to look forward to. Use AI to plan one genuinely good day off — no agenda, no obligations, no work data. Try it in ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini (all free):
homework.prompt:
I want to design my ideal Saturday with zero obligations. Before suggesting anything, ask me 3 questions about what actually recharges me — whether that's quiet, adventure, people, or none of the above.

Then give me a loose hour-by-hour plan for the day, with one option that costs nothing and one small 'treat yourself' upgrade.

2026-06-23.intro: Tuesday. Three new data points this week confirm what most leaders already sense: AI is forcing a rethink of how work is organized -- not just what tools are on the menu.

trends.title: Leadership alignment is the real AI bottleneck
trends.body: Microsoft's 2026 Work Trend Index found that 82% of leaders call 2026 a pivotal year to rethink strategy and operations -- but only 1 in 4 AI users say leadership is consistently aligned on what AI should do. The gap isn't tooling; it's direction.

slants.strategy: If your AI investments aren't paired with a clear owner and measurable outcome, they're experiments, not strategy -- and experiments don't show up in next year's budget.
slants.operations: Misaligned leadership means mixed signals for the people actually running the tools -- define what good AI use looks like on your team so your staff isn't guessing.
slants.pm: Leadership misalignment surfaces in your project -- as scope creep, last-minute AI requests, and post-launch pivots. Surface the question of AI governance in kickoff, not retrospective.
slants.admin: When AI strategy is unclear from the top, frontline staff get asked to figure it out themselves -- your practical experience using these tools is exactly what leadership needs to hear.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.
Paste: Our organization is adopting AI but leadership alignment is weak. What are 3 practical questions a team lead could raise to push for clearer direction?
Pick the one question that feels most relevant to your team.
Ask a follow-up: Rewrite that question as a 2-sentence message I could send to my manager before our next 1:1.
Share your question in the community group.

trends.title: AI agents are now an enterprise infrastructure problem
trends.body: Gartner projects that 40% of enterprise applications will include task-specific AI agents by the end of 2026, up from fewer than 5% in 2025. Microsoft shipped its Work IQ APIs (June 16) -- signaling that agents are moving from pilot to plumbing.

slants.strategy: Every agent your organization deploys is an infrastructure commitment -- before approving the next one, ask who owns it and what happens when it makes a mistake.
slants.operations: The people who feel agent sprawl first are ops -- start designing the role of who monitors agents and catches edge cases now.
slants.pm: Agent integrations belong in your project charter, not your backlog -- scope includes who monitors the agent and what the rollback plan is.
slants.admin: As agents handle more routing and drafting, the value you add shifts to quality control and judgment -- make sure you know what the agents in your stack are doing each day.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.
Paste: What is an AI agent in plain language? Give me 3 examples a typical office professional might already be using without realizing it.
Identify whether any of those sound familiar in your own work.
Ask a follow-up: For one of those agent types, what are 3 questions I should ask before trusting its output at work?
Share one agent you recognize in your workflow in the community group.

trends.title: The job market is splitting -- and human skills are winning
trends.body: PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer found that roles where AI elevates human judgment are seeing twice the job growth and 42% faster salary growth than roles where AI simply handles the work.

slants.strategy: The competitive talent question isn't who can use AI -- it's who can use AI to think better. Redesign roles around the judgment AI can't replicate.
slants.operations: Process design, exception handling, and cross-functional coordination are growing more valuable -- not less -- as AI handles the routine.
slants.pm: The PM skills AI amplifies most are prioritization, stakeholder alignment, and judgment under uncertainty -- things you build through practice, not prompts.
slants.admin: The admin functions with the strongest outlook require relationship management and contextual judgment -- these are not automatable, and they're increasingly rare.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.
Paste: I work in [your role]. What specific skills in my role are becoming MORE valuable as AI handles routine tasks? Give me 3 with a concrete example of each.
Pick the skill you feel least confident about right now.
Ask a follow-up: Give me one 15-minute practice scenario I can try this week to strengthen that skill -- keep it personal, not work-related.
Share what skill you're focusing on in the community group.

homework.title: Decode your summer utility bill with AI
homework.body: Try this at home: use AI to decode a bill that always feels like a mystery. The technique is called structured output -- you tell the AI exactly what sections you want back. Try this in ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini (all free).
homework.prompt: I'm going to paste my electric or gas bill. Please analyze it and give me: (1) a plain-English summary of what I'm paying for, (2) the 2-3 biggest cost drivers this month, (3) one practical thing I can do to reduce the bill without major changes, and (4) any charges or fees I should question. Here is my bill: [paste your bill text here]

2026-06-24.intro: Wednesday. This week's AI signals are pointing in one direction: the professionals treating AI as a full workflow partner — not just a single-task helper — are unlocking a level of output that's changing what "good work" looks like. The opportunity to be one of them is wide open.

trends.title: Your AI Tool Can Now Run the Whole Play
trends.body: The biggest shift in AI productivity in 2026 isn't a new model — it's a new mode of working. Today's leading AI platforms don't just answer questions; they complete entire workflows: researching, drafting, formatting, and delivering — all in one pass. The AI productivity landscape has shifted decisively from single-task assistants to multi-step agents capable of owning full workflows from research through final output, with over 20 major platforms releasing agentic features in the first half of 2026, per aisofting.com's 2026 AI Productivity Tools Update. For working professionals, this means the unit of delegation is no longer a prompt — it's an entire process.

slants.strategy: If your team is still using AI one question at a time, you're running a 2024 strategy with 2026 tools — the competitive advantage now lives in delegating whole workflows, not individual tasks.
slants.operations: Full-workflow AI means a process that used to take your team a full day — intake, research, draft, format — can now return a polished output in under an hour, compressing turnaround and freeing capacity for higher-value work.
slants.pm: Think of AI agents as a force multiplier on your projects: research briefs, status summaries, and stakeholder updates can now be produced autonomously, letting you focus on the decisions and relationships that need your judgment.
slants.admin: Multi-step AI means you can hand off a complex support task — travel research, meeting prep, briefing documents — and get back a finished, ready-to-share result, making your contribution feel more strategic than ever.

steps: Identify one recurring multi-step task you do weekly — something that involves research, then writing or summarizing a result.

Open your AI tool and paste this prompt: "You are my expert assistant. Complete this task from start to finish: [describe your task in 1-2 sentences]. First, gather the key information. Then organize the main points. Then write the final output."
Review the full output — note where it landed well and where it missed the mark.
Run this follow-up to refine: "Revise the output with these changes: [your specific edits]. Format the final version as [your preferred format — bullet list, email, one-pager, etc.]."
Save your two-prompt sequence as a reusable template for this task going forward.

trends.title: AI Meeting Notes Just Crossed the Unicorn Line
trends.body: The AI meeting intelligence category just had its defining moment. Granola — which automatically captures, structures, and summarizes your meetings — closed a $125M Series C and reached a $1.5 billion valuation, up from $250 million just one year earlier, according to TechCrunch (March 2026). The message from investors and enterprises alike: AI-powered meeting context is becoming foundational infrastructure, not a productivity add-on.

slants.strategy: A $1.5B bet on meeting intelligence signals that the companies winning right now treat meeting context as a strategic asset — if your team's decisions are still scattered across personal notes and inboxes, now is the time to centralize.
slants.operations: AI meeting tools close the gap between what was decided and what actually gets done — every action item, owner, and deadline surfaces automatically, making follow-through a default rather than an extra effort.
slants.pm: When AI captures every meeting decision and next step automatically, your status updates practically write themselves and your projects move faster — this is leverage that makes you look like you have more bandwidth than anyone else in the room.
slants.admin: AI meeting tools transform the admin role from manual transcription to strategic review — you're distributing clean summaries and clear action items, a visible upgrade that showcases your value to the whole team.

steps: Before your next meeting, open your AI tool and have it ready — you'll use it right after.

After the meeting, paste your raw notes with this prompt: "Here are my notes from a [type of meeting] meeting. Please extract: (1) key decisions made, (2) action items with owners, (3) open questions still unresolved, and (4) a 3-sentence summary. [paste notes here]"
Review the output and correct any misattributions or items that were missed.
Follow up with: "Format this as a brief recap message I can send to the attendees. Keep it under 150 words and use a professional but warm tone."
Send the recap (or save it as your post-meeting template) — and track how much time you just recovered.

trends.title: Enterprise AI Spending Just Hit Warp Speed
trends.body: The numbers behind enterprise AI adoption in 2026 are now hard to ignore. Anthropic's annualized revenue surpassed $30 billion — up from approximately $9 billion just six months earlier — with over 1,000 businesses now each spending more than $1 million annually, a number that doubled in under two months, per Anthropic. When organizations are committing at this scale, AI fluency has moved from a résumé bonus to a baseline professional expectation.

slants.strategy: Revenue growth at this speed signals that enterprise AI adoption has crossed into the mainstream — strategy leaders who haven't built AI into their operating model aren't early movers anymore, but there's still real advantage in moving now.
slants.operations: As your organization's AI tools become more capable and embedded, the ops professional who already knows how to leverage them becomes the go-to resource — positioning you ahead of the next wave before it arrives.
slants.pm: As AI becomes standard across enterprise stacks, PMs who can define AI-augmented workflows will have a decisive advantage in velocity, quality, and the confidence of their stakeholders.
slants.admin: The faster enterprise AI adoption accelerates, the more valuable the admin who already knows how to use these tools well becomes — now is the moment to build that expertise and let it be visible.

steps: Choose one deliverable you produce regularly — a report, brief, summary, or proposal — and note how long it currently takes you from scratch.
Open your AI tool and try this prompt: "Act as an expert in [your field or role]. Draft a [deliverable type] based on this input: [paste your source material, bullet points, or key facts here]."
Time how long it takes from first prompt to a polished draft — compare it to your usual time.
Refine with: "Tighten the language, cut anything repetitive, and add one specific example or stat to strengthen the main point."
Track your time savings across three uses of this workflow, then share the result with your manager as a case for formalizing the process.

homework.title: Plan Your Weekend Menu in Five Minutes
homework.body: Tonight's homework is a low-stakes way to practice structured output — the prompting technique where you tell AI exactly what format and categories you want in the response. It's one of the highest-leverage skills you can build, because it transfers directly to any work task where you need organized information fast: briefings, comparison tables, action plans, project summaries. Use your fridge as the dataset and see how precisely AI organizes to your specs.
homework.prompt: Act as a practical home meal planner. Based on these ingredients and constraints: [paste what's in your fridge plus any dietary preferences or time limits], create a weekend plan — Saturday dinner and Sunday brunch — that requires no special shopping. Format each meal as: Meal name | Key ingredients | Cook time | One reason we'll love it.

2026-06-25.intro: Thursday. This week's biggest AI moves are all about the ecosystem growing up: new career tracks, smarter agents, and policy clarity that lets your organization say yes to AI with confidence.

trends.title: A New AI Career Track Just Opened — At Scale
trends.body: OpenAI launched its Partner Network with a $150 million commitment targeting 300,000 certified AI consultants by the end of 2026 — a credentialing wave roughly the size of Accenture's entire global workforce (OpenAI, June 14). The three-tier program (Select, Advanced, Elite) adds specializations in AI agents, Codex, and cybersecurity. It's the fastest professional AI credentialing expansion on record.

slants.strategy: If your team gets into this ecosystem early — as a certified implementer or an informed buyer — you're positioned ahead of the 300K wave, not swept along in it.
slants.operations: Certified AI partners mean you'll have access to a vetted network of implementers when it's time to scale beyond pilot — understanding the tier structure helps you choose the right partner faster.
slants.pm: Enterprise AI projects are increasingly delivered through certified partner networks — knowing the Select, Advanced, and Elite tier distinctions helps you scope vendor relationships and set realistic delivery expectations.
slants.admin: Certifications like these are how organizations validate AI competence — now is a great time to explore whether there's a credential path worth pursuing in your own role, even informally.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.

Paste: "What is a professional certification program, and why do organizations create them? Give me 3 reasons why OpenAI might want to certify 300,000 consultants rather than just publish free documentation."
Pick the reason that resonates most with your industry or organization.
Ask a follow-up: "What would I need to know or be able to do to add genuine value as an AI-informed professional working alongside one of these certified consultants?"
Share one skill or knowledge area you want to build in the community group.

trends.title: AI Agents Are Resolving Three-Quarters of Support Tickets — Automatically
trends.body: Salesforce announced a $3.6 billion acquisition of Fin, an AI customer service platform, on June 15. Fin's AI Agent already resolves 76% of customer support volume end-to-end across chat, email, SMS, WhatsApp, and phone (Salesforce/TechCrunch). The deal fast-tracks Salesforce's Agentforce strategy, adding an agent layer that SMBs and enterprise teams can deploy quickly.

slants.strategy: When AI handles the routine, every human interaction becomes a premium signal — now is the time to define which customer moments are worth keeping human and build your team's skills there.
slants.operations: A 76% resolution rate means your operations team's energy shifts from ticket triage to exception design — the people who thrive are those who can define when the agent should hand off to a human.
slants.pm: If Salesforce is in your stack, Agentforce plus Fin is coming to your roadmap — get ahead of it by asking your team now how handoff logic between AI agents and humans should work.
slants.admin: As AI handles routine requests, your role becomes the escalation and relationship layer — the moments that actually matter to people. That's not a smaller role; it's a more visible one.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.
Paste: "I work in [your field or team]. What are the 3 most repetitive requests or tasks that come to my team or to me? Which of these could a well-designed AI agent realistically handle?"
Pick one task from the list the AI identified.
Ask a follow-up: "If an AI agent handled that task automatically, what is the most important thing a human still needs to review or follow up on — and why?"
Share the task you identified in the community group.

trends.title: Washington Just Made It Easier to Say Yes to Enterprise AI
trends.body: On June 2, the White House signed an executive order establishing a voluntary framework for frontier AI development and a new AI Cybersecurity Clearinghouse. The order explicitly prohibits mandatory licensing or pre-clearance requirements for AI models (White House), a deliberate policy choice to accelerate adoption. Enterprises now have a clear signal: build.

slants.strategy: No mandatory licensing means the investment climate for enterprise AI stays open — organizations that treat this window as a build moment will be running when others are still evaluating.
slants.operations: The AI Cybersecurity Clearinghouse means coordinated vulnerability tracking across industries — a practical step toward the security baseline that makes it easier to get AI tools approved internally.
slants.pm: Policy stability reduces the risk profile of long-horizon AI projects — if deals have stalled on 'we need to wait and see what regulators do,' this is a meaningful signal to bring back to the table.
slants.admin: A voluntary framework means your organization has real latitude to move — and real responsibility to set its own standards. Being fluent in your company's AI policies is increasingly a strategic skill.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.

Paste: "In plain language, what is the difference between a mandatory regulation and a voluntary framework for AI? What are 2 advantages and 1 risk of the voluntary approach for a typical mid-sized company?"
Think about how your own organization approaches AI — does it wait for rules, or move ahead of them?
Ask a follow-up: "What are 3 questions I should ask my IT or compliance team to understand how our organization's AI policies are structured right now?"
Share one question from the list in the community group.

homework.title: Plan a weekend trip in 10 minutes with AI
homework.body: Try this at home: use AI to plan a quick weekend getaway using a technique called chain prompting — where each answer feeds your next question. This builds the habit of treating AI as a thinking partner rather than a search engine. Try it in ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini (all free).
homework.prompt: I want to plan a quick weekend trip. Here are my constraints: [budget range, departure city, time of year, any must-haves or must-avoids]. Please give me: (1) 3 destination options that fit, (2) one specific activity for each, (3) a Friday-to-Sunday rough itinerary for my top pick, and (4) one thing I should book in advance and one I can leave flexible.

2026-06-26.intro: Friday. This week's biggest AI moves are all about infrastructure catching up to ambition — a new chip, a new agent inside your existing tools, and new data confirming the time savings are real. The question now is what you do with them.

trends.title: Your AI Tools Just Got a Speed and Cost Upgrade
trends.body: OpenAI and Broadcom announced Jalapeño on June 24 — OpenAI's first custom AI inference chip, built from scratch in just nine months. Designed specifically for large language models, the chip delivers performance per watt substantially better than today's state-of-the-art, with gigawatt-scale data center deployments beginning before end of 2026 (OpenAI/Broadcom). When the infrastructure powering your AI tools gets faster and cheaper, so does everything you build with it.

slants.strategy: Faster inference and lower infrastructure costs mean AI capabilities that only large enterprises could afford in 2025 will be accessible to mid-market teams within 12 months — plan for capability jumps, not just cost reductions.
slants.operations: Speed-optimized AI infrastructure means the latency issues that make some AI workflows impractical today are likely to disappear — revisit automations you ruled out as too slow.
slants.pm: When AI gets faster and cheaper, scope conversations change — projects that required phasing AI in incrementally can now be designed with AI-first flows from the start.
slants.admin: Infrastructure advances like this don't require you to do anything — but they're worth knowing, because they're why the AI tools you use today will feel noticeably more capable six months from now.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.
Paste: "What is an AI inference chip, and why does having a custom one matter for a company like OpenAI? Explain it in terms a non-technical professional would understand."
Note one thing that surprised you or changed how you think about how AI tools actually work.
Ask a follow-up: "If AI inference gets twice as fast and half as expensive in the next 12 months, what are 3 ways a typical mid-sized company might change how it uses AI?"
Share one of the three ideas in the community group.

trends.title: Microsoft's New AI Agent Works While You Sleep
trends.body: Microsoft unveiled Scout this week — an always-on AI agent built into Microsoft 365 that autonomously handles multi-step tasks across Teams, Outlook, calendars, OneDrive, and SharePoint without waiting for step-by-step instructions. Scout is designed to coordinate schedules, surface workflow bottlenecks, and carry out routine activities autonomously across the Microsoft stack (Microsoft, June 2026). For the hundreds of millions of professionals already in M365, it's not a new tool to learn — it's a layer inside the tools you already use every day.

slants.strategy: Scout isn't a productivity add-on — it's a signal that Microsoft is repositioning M365 as an autonomous work platform. Organizations that define what Scout should and shouldn't do now will shape how their teams work, rather than react to it.
slants.operations: An autonomous agent that surfaces bottlenecks and coordinates across systems is exactly what an operations function needs — the teams that figure out how to direct Scout's attention will compound their throughput significantly.
slants.pm: Scout can monitor project channels, flag stalled threads, and draft status updates automatically — the PMs who learn to define its parameters well will spend less time chasing and more time leading.
