Try a multi-step follow-up: "Now draft a follow-up email to the team summarizing these action items in a friendly, professional tone. Keep it under 150 words."
Send or schedule the email and note how much time the two-prompt workflow saved you compared to your usual process.

homework.title: Design Your Dream Room with AI
homework.body: This one's purely for fun — and for practicing structured prompting with rich context. Give AI a detailed picture of your style, constraints, and wish list to generate a room redesign or home decor plan you'd actually love. You're building the same skill that makes your work prompts dramatically more specific and useful.
homework.prompt: You are an interior designer. Using the information below, suggest a complete redesign for my [room type] — including color palette, furniture arrangement, lighting ideas, and 3 specific styles or pieces to look for. My current setup: [describe it]. My style preferences: [describe your taste]. My budget: [range]. My biggest pain point with this room: [what bothers you most about it].

2026-07-07.intro: Tuesday. Today's three stories have a common thread — the gap between knowing about AI and actually getting results from it is closing fastest for the people and companies that aren't waiting for permission.

trends.title: 20% of companies are capturing three-quarters of AI's economic value
trends.body: PwC's 2026 AI Performance Study found a stark concentration: 74% of AI's economic gains are being captured by just 20% of organizations. The difference isn't budget or technology access — it's structure. AI leaders are 1.7 times more likely to have a responsible AI framework, 1.5 times more likely to have a cross-functional governance board, and their employees are twice as likely to trust AI outputs (PwC, 2026).

slants.strategy: The strategic differentiator isn't which AI tools you buy — it's whether leadership has organized the company to actually use them. Governance sounds boring until it's the reason your competitor moves twice as fast.
slants.operations: AI leaders don't just deploy tools — they redesign how decisions get made. If your team still routes every AI output through the same approval chain as manual work, you're leaving speed on the table.
slants.pm: Projects in AI-leading organizations get clearer guardrails upfront, which means less rework and fewer stalled approvals mid-stream. Push for those guardrails in your next kickoff.
slants.admin: When leadership trusts AI outputs, the review-and-forward cycle that eats your day gets shorter. Advocate for clear guidelines on what AI-assisted work needs a second look and what doesn't.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool and paste this prompt: "I work at a large organization that uses AI tools but doesn't have a formal AI governance framework. List the 5 most important elements of an AI governance plan, explained in plain English for a non-technical audience."
Read the list and identify which elements your organization already has — even informally.
Pick the one missing element that would make the biggest difference for your team's daily work.
Draft a one-paragraph recommendation you could share with your manager or team lead.
Post your recommendation in the group — you might inspire someone else to start the same conversation.

trends.title: AI super-users are getting promoted 3x faster — and they're not all technical
trends.body: Writer's 2026 enterprise survey of 2,400 employees and executives found that AI super-users are three times more likely to have received a raise or promotion in the past year — and report five times higher productivity (Writer/Workplace Intelligence, April 2026). Meanwhile, 75% of executives admit their company's AI strategy is "more for show" than actual guidance. The people getting ahead aren't waiting for a formal program.

slants.strategy: The competitive advantage isn't the AI budget — it's the learning culture. Teams where individuals experiment on their own are outpacing teams waiting for top-down direction.
slants.operations: You don't need a formal training program to start. The ops professionals pulling ahead are the ones testing AI on one real task per week — consistency beats curriculum.
slants.pm: Project managers who understand AI capabilities write better requirements and set more realistic timelines. That shows up in performance reviews even when nobody calls it an AI skill.
slants.admin: Every challenge you've completed in this group counts. Consistent, low-stakes practice is the difference between "I've heard of AI" and "I use it every day."

steps: Think of one task you completed this week where AI could have helped — even partially.
Open your company-approved AI tool and try it: describe the task and ask for help with the specific part that took the most time.
Compare the result to what you did manually. Was it faster? Better? At least a useful starting point?
Log this as your AI experiment of the week and share what you tried in the group — including what worked and what didn't.

trends.title: Non-technical teams are building their own AI workflows — and it's working
trends.body: Gartner reports that 41% of employees in large enterprises are now "business technologists" — people outside IT who build tech or analytics capabilities for their own teams. With 75% of new enterprise applications expected to be built on low-code platforms by year-end, the tools have caught up to the ambition. You don't need to write code to automate your own work anymore.

slants.strategy: The planning bottleneck used to be IT capacity. Now your team can prototype a workflow in hours — the strategy-to-execution gap just got dramatically shorter.
slants.operations: If you've ever wished someone would build a tool to automate that one annoying recurring process — you are that someone now.
slants.pm: Low-code tools let your team test solutions during discovery instead of waiting for a development sprint. Faster validation, fewer wasted cycles.
slants.admin: The next time you think "there should be an app for this," check whether your company's no-code tools can build it. You'll surprise yourself.

steps: Identify one small, repetitive task you wish were automated — a recurring report, a status check, a form you fill out every week.

Open your company-approved AI tool and ask: "I want to automate this task: [describe it]. What's the simplest way to do this using a no-code or low-code tool? Assume I have no programming experience and walk me through it step by step."
Read the suggested approach and note whether any of the steps feel doable this week.
Share your task and the AI's suggestion in the group — someone else might have already solved it.

homework.title: Let AI help you tackle that thing you've been putting off.
homework.body: Everyone has one — the errand, the phone call, the decision that keeps sliding to tomorrow. Tonight, use any free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity) to break it into something manageable.
homework.prompt:
"I've been putting off [describe what you've been avoiding]. I think the main reason is [your best guess — it feels overwhelming, I don't know where to start, I'm dreading the conversation, etc.]. Break this into 3 small, specific steps I could start tonight. For each step, tell me exactly how long it should take and why it's easier than I think."

The technique here — giving AI your reasoning about why you're stuck, not just what you're stuck on — gets you advice that actually fits your situation instead of generic tips.

2026-07-08.intro: Wednesday. Today's three stories share a pattern — AI is reshaping careers, organizations, and confidence at different speeds. The good news: the skills you're building right now are exactly the ones the market rewards most.

trends.title: The labor market is splitting into two tracks — and judgment-heavy roles are winning
trends.body: PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer analyzed over one billion job ads across 27 countries and found AI is creating a clear divide. Professionalized roles — where AI handles routine tasks and human judgment becomes the core value — are seeing twice the job growth and 42% faster salary growth than roles where AI simply makes the work easier for non-experts (PwC, June 2026). Entry-level positions in AI-exposed fields are now seven times more likely to require traditionally senior skills like leadership and strategic thinking.

slants.strategy: Your role is on the professionalized track — the one with faster growth and higher pay. The premium is on judgment, not execution. Double down on framing the right question, reading the room, and knowing when the model is wrong.
slants.operations: The ops skills that matter most are shifting from process execution to process design. Running a workflow is table stakes — knowing how to redesign one around AI is the differentiator.
slants.pm: AI-exposed PM roles are demanding more strategic thinking and less task tracking. The project managers pulling ahead are the ones who can evaluate AI outputs, not just assign them.
slants.admin: The wage premium for AI skills hit 62% in PwC's data. Every time you practice with these tools — including right now — you're building the skills the market is paying more for.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool and paste this prompt: "List the top 5 skills in my role ([your role]) that AI cannot replicate. For each, give me one specific way I could demonstrate or strengthen that skill this month."
Review the list and identify the one skill you use most often but rarely get credit for.
Ask a follow-up: "Write a short talking point I could use in a performance review to explain why this skill is more valuable now that AI handles the routine parts of my job."
Save the talking point — and share the skill you identified in the group.

trends.title: AI agents are scaling faster than the guardrails around them
trends.body: Deloitte's 2026 State of AI in the Enterprise survey of 3,235 senior leaders found autonomous AI agents racing into production — 85% of companies plan to customize agents for their business, and 74% expect at least moderate agent use by 2027 (Deloitte, 2026). The catch: only one in five companies has a mature governance model for autonomous AI. Agents are already rebooking flights, drafting post-meeting communications, and supporting product development — but the oversight frameworks haven't kept pace.

slants.strategy: The governance gap is a strategic risk, not just a compliance issue. If your organization is deploying agents without clear rules for when they act autonomously and when they need human sign-off, you're building speed on a shaky foundation.
slants.operations: Agents are already handling real workflows — rebooking, communications, supply chain decisions. The ops question isn't whether to use them, it's whether you have checkpoints for when they make the wrong call.
slants.pm: Add 'agent oversight' as a standing item in any project that uses AI automation. Who reviews the agent's decisions? What happens when it's wrong? Answer before deployment, not after.
slants.admin: You're likely already interacting with AI agents — chatbots, automated scheduling, smart routing. Knowing which ones are making decisions on your behalf versus just assisting is the first step to working with them confidently.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool and ask: "Explain the difference between an AI assistant and an AI agent in plain English, with one real-world example of each that a non-technical office professional would encounter."
Think about the AI tools you use daily. For each one, decide: is it assisting me (I make the decisions) or acting for me (it makes decisions I review after)?
For any tool that acts on your behalf, write down what guardrails are in place — and what's missing.
Share your assistant-versus-agent split in the group — you'll be surprised how many agents are already in your workflow.

trends.title: Workers are using AI more — but trusting themselves less
trends.body: ManpowerGroup's 2026 Global Talent Barometer found a striking paradox: regular AI usage jumped 13 points to 45% of workers globally, yet confidence in using technology fell 18% — the first overall confidence decline in three years (ManpowerGroup, 2026). The result is what researchers call 'job hugging': 64% of workers plan to stay with their current employer, driven by a desire for stability rather than loyalty. Meanwhile, 56% report receiving no recent training and 57% have no access to mentorship.

slants.strategy: Surface-level retention numbers can mask an anxious workforce. If your team's tenure looks stable but engagement surveys tell a different story, 'job hugging' is likely hiding a flight risk that surfaces the moment the market shifts.
slants.operations: The training gap is the actionable finding: more than half of workers got no recent training. Even informal shared prompt libraries or 15-minute brown-bag sessions close this gap faster than waiting for a formal program.
slants.pm: If your team seems hesitant to adopt new tools, this data suggests it's confidence, not resistance. Build learning time into the sprint and normalize asking questions about AI — it changes the dynamic.
slants.admin: You're in this community — which means you're already ahead of the 56% who got no training at all. Every challenge you've tried here is real practice that compounds over time.

steps: Rate your own AI confidence honestly, 1 to 10. No one's watching.

Open any AI tool and try this prompt: "I'm a [your role] who's been using AI tools for a few months. Give me three specific things I can practice this week to move from 'I can use AI' to 'I'm confident teaching someone else.' Keep each under two sentences."
Pick the one that feels most doable and try it before Friday.
Share your confidence rating and your one practice goal in the group — honesty helps everyone.

homework.title: Tonight, let AI help you draft that message you've been putting off.
homework.body: Everyone has one — the text you keep meaning to send, the email you've rewritten in your head a dozen times, the reply sitting in drafts. Use any free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity) to get past the blank-page paralysis.
homework.prompt:
"I need to [describe the message — e.g., tell a friend I can't make their event / ask my landlord about a repair / follow up on something awkward]. The reason I've been putting it off is [your honest reason]. Write three versions: one direct, one gentle, and one that uses a little humor. Keep each under 4 sentences. Then tell me which one you'd recommend and why."

The technique here — asking for multiple versions ranked by tone — teaches you that the first draft is never the only option. Works for work emails, tricky conversations, and negotiations too.

2026-07-09.intro: Thursday. Three stories today about the same uncomfortable truth: most organizations are confusing AI access with AI readiness — and the people who see the difference are pulling ahead.

trends.title: Giving people AI tools isn't the same as enabling them to use AI well
trends.body: Gartner's 2026 Global Labor Market Survey of 12,004 employees across 40 countries uncovered what they call the 'enablement illusion': most leaders mistake basic adoption metrics — tool logins, license counts — for real workforce transformation. The gap matters. Employees proficient with AI across multiple use cases are twice as productive, 2.3 times more likely to deliver high-quality work, and 3.2 times more likely to drive effective process improvements. Access isn't the bottleneck. Competence is.

slants.strategy: If your AI success metric is 'how many people have access,' you're measuring the wrong thing. Shift to outcomes: decisions improved, cycle times shortened, quality gains documented.
slants.operations: The 2x productivity gap between casual and proficient AI users means your team's ROI depends on depth of skill, not breadth of rollout.
slants.pm: Two project teams with identical AI tools can deliver wildly different results. The variable isn't the technology — it's how well each team actually knows how to use it.
slants.admin: Every time you practice a new prompt technique or try a different approach, you're widening the gap between 'has access' and 'gets results.' That gap is where the value lives.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool and paste this prompt: "Rate my AI proficiency based on this conversation. Ask me 5 quick questions about how I use AI at work — what tools, what tasks, how often, what I do with the output. Then give me an honest rating from 1 (beginner) to 5 (proficient) and one specific skill to develop next."
Answer the five questions honestly — there's no wrong answer.
Read the rating and the suggested next skill. Does it match your self-assessment?
Share your rating and the skill suggestion in the group — comparing notes helps everyone calibrate.

trends.title: Half of organizations still don't train non-technical employees on AI
trends.body: Forrester's AIQ 2.0 report measured workforce AI readiness across six countries and found the training gap is barely closing. Only 51% of organizations offer AI training to non-technical employees — up just four points from a year earlier. Prompt engineering training is even rarer: just 23% of firms offer it. The result: only 16% of employees achieved a high AI Quotient score, and fewer than four in ten feel confident adapting to AI-driven work.

slants.strategy: If your organization is in the half that doesn't train non-technical staff, every AI investment is underperforming by design. The tool isn't the bottleneck — the missing training is.
slants.operations: Low-AIQ employees don't just adopt slowly — they introduce errors and rework. The cost of not training is showing up in your quality metrics, not just your adoption dashboards.
slants.pm: Before kicking off an AI-integrated project, ask one question: has this team been trained? If the answer is no, build learning time into the plan or accept the rework risk.
slants.admin: Being in this community puts you in the top half. Most organizations haven't offered their non-technical teams any AI training at all. What you're learning here counts.

steps: Think of one AI skill you've picked up in the last month — a prompting trick, a workflow shortcut, a way to check AI output for accuracy.

Open your AI tool and ask: "I just learned how to [describe the skill]. Write me a 3-minute explanation I could give to a colleague who's never tried this. Keep it simple, include one example, and end with 'try it right now with [specific task].'"
Review the explanation. Edit anything that doesn't sound like you.
Send it to one colleague who hasn't started using AI yet — or share it in the group so others can borrow the format.

trends.title: Employees are ready for AI — their leaders aren't keeping up
trends.body: McKinsey's State of Organizations 2026 report surveyed over 10,000 respondents and found a leadership readiness gap holding organizations back. 86% of leaders say their organization is not prepared to integrate AI into day-to-day operations, and only 14% say leadership is consistently championing adoption. Meanwhile, 47% of C-suite executives admit their companies are moving too slowly — and the top reason isn't technology or budget. It's talent skill gaps.

slants.strategy: The bottleneck isn't your team's willingness — it's leadership clarity. If there's no clear owner of AI strategy at the top, every team is improvising independently.
slants.operations: When leadership hasn't set clear AI guidelines, ops teams default to the safest option: don't use it. That caution is rational, but it's costing you speed.
slants.pm: If your project's AI adoption feels stalled, look up before you look down. The blocker is more likely a missing mandate from leadership than resistance from the team.
slants.admin: If you've been waiting for permission to use AI at work, here's the pattern: leaders are slower to move than their teams. Find the approved tools and start practicing — don't wait for a formal announcement.

steps: Open your AI tool and try this prompt: "I work in [your role] at a large organization. Leadership hasn't given clear guidance on when and how to use AI in our daily work. Write me three polite, professional questions I could ask my manager to get clarity — covering which tools are approved, what tasks are appropriate for AI, and how AI-assisted work should be reviewed."
Read the three questions. Pick the one most relevant to your situation right now.
Decide: will you ask it this week? If not, what's holding you back?
Share which question you picked — and whether you plan to ask it — in the group.

homework.title: Tonight, let AI teach you something you've always meant to learn.
homework.body: We all have that topic — how mortgage rates actually work, what compound interest really does, how to read a nutrition label, why the electoral college exists. Use any free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity) and try this:
homework.prompt:
"I've always wanted to properly understand [your topic]. Explain it to me like I'm smart but have zero background. Start with the one core idea I need to grasp first, then build from there. After each section, check if I'm following before moving on."

That last instruction — check if I'm following before moving on — turns a monologue into a conversation. It's one of the most effective prompting techniques for learning anything new.

2026-07-10.intro: Friday. Three stories to carry into the weekend — about the hidden biases in your AI's advice, why most AI projects fail for reasons that have nothing to do with technology, and the quiet skill that separates people who use AI from people who get real value from it.

trends.title: Your AI gives everyone the same strategic advice — and nobody notices
trends.body: Harvard Business Review published research in March testing seven leading AI models across 15,000 strategy simulations. The result: a phenomenon the researchers call 'trendslop' — when asked to choose between competing strategic approaches, the models clustered around the same trendy recommendations regardless of context. Even when researchers changed industries, company sizes, and constraints, the AI kept recommending the same buzzword-heavy playbooks. The advice sounds confident. It's just not specific to you.

slants.strategy: If your AI-generated strategy document could apply to any company in any industry, it's trendslop. The value isn't in the generation — it's in the pressure-testing.
slants.operations: Generic AI recommendations for process improvement miss the constraints that make your operations unique. Always feed it your actual bottlenecks, not a textbook description.
slants.pm: When AI suggests a project approach, ask yourself: would this same advice work for a completely different project? If yes, it's too generic to act on without heavy tailoring.
slants.admin: The more specific you are in your prompts — names, dates, constraints, history — the less likely AI is to hand you something generic. Context is the antidote to trendslop.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool and paste this prompt: "I need strategic advice on [describe a real challenge you're working on — a process that's too slow, a decision you're stuck on, a project that's stalling]. Give me your recommendation."
Read the response. Then ask this follow-up: "Now argue against your own recommendation. What are the three strongest reasons this advice might be wrong for my specific situation?"
Compare the two responses. Which one gave you more useful insight — the recommendation or the counterargument?
Share what you found in the group — did the AI's self-critique surface something the original advice missed?

trends.title: 80% of enterprise AI projects fail — and the fix isn't better technology
trends.body: RAND Corporation's meta-analysis of 65+ enterprise AI initiatives and Gartner's April 2026 survey of 782 I&O managers converge on the same conclusion: more than 80% of enterprise AI projects fail to deliver promised business value. Only 28% of AI infrastructure projects deliver the return that was expected. The root cause isn't the models or the tools — it's change management. Teams expected AI to automate complex tasks without redesigning the work around it.

slants.strategy: The next strategy deck that proposes an AI initiative should spend as much time on workflow redesign and change management as it does on technology selection.
slants.operations: If your team bolted AI onto an existing process and it didn't stick, that's not an AI failure — it's a design failure. The process needs to change, not just the tools inside it.
slants.pm: Build change management into every AI project plan from day one — training, workflow redesign, feedback loops. The projects that skip this step are the ones in the 80%.
slants.admin: When a new AI tool doesn't seem to help, the issue is often that nobody redesigned the steps around it. You're in a good position to spot where the old process and the new tool conflict.

steps: Think of one AI tool or feature your team has tried in the last six months. Did it stick? Be honest.

Open your AI tool and paste this prompt: "We tried using AI for [describe the task], but adoption was [low / uneven / abandoned]. Walk me through a 5-step diagnostic: what might have gone wrong with the rollout, the workflow design, the training, the expectations, or the feedback loop? Be specific and practical."
Review the diagnostic. Identify the one factor that rings most true for your situation.
Share your diagnosis in the group — patterns across teams tell us more than any single case.

trends.title: The new edge isn't using AI — it's teaching AI how you think
trends.body: A June 2026 Harvard Business Review article argues that the real bottleneck for AI value has shifted. It's no longer access, skills, or even organizational readiness — it's whether you can make your decision-making process explicit. Many critical judgments about risk, exceptions, quality, and tradeoffs exist as tacit knowledge inside experienced employees. Organizations that translate that knowledge into structured guidance create what the authors call 'judgment infrastructure' — and it scales. Everyone else gets generic output.

slants.strategy: Your strategic value increasingly lives in the judgment calls you make — the ones that aren't written down anywhere. Making them explicit doesn't replace you. It amplifies you.
slants.operations: Every time you make an exception, apply a rule of thumb, or escalate based on instinct, that's tacit knowledge. Documenting it — even roughly — makes AI dramatically more useful for your team.
slants.pm: The project managers who get the best AI output are the ones who can articulate their decision criteria upfront. 'Use your judgment' is a human instruction. AI needs the criteria spelled out.
slants.admin: You already have deep tacit knowledge — the unwritten rules of how things actually work, who to loop in, when to escalate. That knowledge is exactly what makes AI more useful when you share it.

steps: Pick one recurring decision you make at work — an approval, a prioritization, a judgment call about quality or urgency.

Open your AI tool and try this prompt: "I'm going to teach you how I make a specific decision. The decision is: [describe it]. Interview me about my decision-making process — ask me 5 questions about what factors I weigh, what exceptions I make, and what rules of thumb I use. After my answers, summarize my decision framework in a simple checklist."
Answer the five questions as honestly as you can — include the shortcuts and instincts, not just the official process.
Review the checklist the AI produces. Is it accurate? What did it miss? What did it capture that you'd never written down before?
Share one insight from the exercise in the group — what piece of your tacit knowledge surprised you when you saw it written out?

homework.title: This weekend, let AI interview you about a skill you take for granted.
homework.body: You're better at something than you realize — cooking, navigating family dynamics, organizing a trip, fixing things around the house. Use any free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity) to surface what you actually know:
homework.prompt:
"I'm really good at [your skill — e.g., meal planning on a budget, defusing arguments, packing for trips]. Interview me about HOW I do it. Ask me 5 questions about my process, then write up my approach as a step-by-step guide that someone else could follow."

The technique here — getting AI to interview you instead of instruct you — flips the usual dynamic. You're the expert. AI is the writer. That's a prompting pattern worth practicing.

2026-07-11.intro: Saturday. Three stories to sit with this weekend — about the changing shape of organizations, a coming correction for AI agent hype, and the surprising disconnect between the people who buy AI tools and the people who use them.

trends.title: AI is flattening the org chart — and redefining what managers actually do
trends.body: Gartner predicts that by the end of 2026, 20% of organizations will use AI to flatten their structure, eliminating more than half of current middle management positions. Companies like Shopify and Klarna have already started. But this isn't a story about managers disappearing — it's about which management tasks survive. Coordination, status-tracking, and report consolidation are being automated. Coaching, cross-functional judgment, and decision-making under ambiguity are not.

slants.strategy: The strategic planning role gets more valuable in a flatter org — not less. Fewer management layers mean fewer filters between strategy and execution.
slants.operations: If your day is mostly coordination and reporting, that's the part AI automates first. If it's mostly judgment calls and exception handling, you're on the side that gains leverage.
slants.pm: In flatter organizations, project managers become the connective tissue. Fewer middle managers means more cross-functional coordination falls to you — and that's a skill AI can't replicate.
slants.admin: As management layers thin, your relationship with senior leaders gets more direct. The institutional knowledge you carry — who to call, how things really work — becomes harder to replace, not easier.

steps: Open a notes app and list the five tasks that take up most of your work week.

Next to each task, write one of three labels: "coordination" (scheduling, status tracking, routing information), "judgment" (decisions, exceptions, evaluations), or "relationship" (coaching, negotiating, persuading).

Look at your split. If most of your time is in coordination, open your AI tool and ask: "I spend most of my work time on coordination tasks like [list them]. Which of these could be partially automated, and what would I do with the freed-up time?"
Share your coordination-judgment-relationship split in the group — the patterns across roles are eye-opening.

trends.title: Nearly half of AI agent projects will be scrapped before they deliver
trends.body: Gartner predicts that more than 40% of agentic AI projects will be canceled by the end of 2027 — due to escalating costs, unclear business value, or inadequate risk controls. Analyst Anushree Verma put it plainly: most agentic AI projects right now are early-stage experiments driven by hype and often misapplied. The projects that survive will be the ones that started with a specific, measurable problem — not a vendor demo.

slants.strategy: Before greenlighting an AI agent initiative, ask one question: what specific decision or workflow does this improve, and how will we measure it? If the answer is vague, the project is already at risk.
slants.operations: The AI agent projects most likely to survive are the ones solving real operational pain — invoice matching, exception routing, pattern detection — not the ones chasing broad 'automation.'
slants.pm: When an AI agent project lands on your plate, treat 'define the success metric' as a non-negotiable first milestone. Projects without a clear baseline are the ones that get canceled.
slants.admin: If someone pitches an AI agent to automate part of your workflow, the best question you can ask is: what specific problem does this solve, and how will we know it's working? That question saves months.

steps: Think of one AI tool or automation your team has tried — or been pitched — in the past six months.

Open your company-approved AI tool and paste this prompt: "We're evaluating an AI project to [describe it]. Play the role of a skeptical CFO. Ask me five tough questions about this project's expected ROI, measurable outcomes, risk controls, and what happens if it fails. Be direct."
Answer the five questions honestly. Which one was hardest to answer?
Share the hardest question — and whether it changed your view of the project — in the group.

trends.title: The people buying AI and the people using it don't agree on what's working
trends.body: Grant Thornton's 2026 AI Impact Survey of 950 business leaders uncovered a striking internal disconnect: 39% of CIOs and CTOs say their workforce is fully ready to adopt AI — but only 7% of COOs agree. Overall, just 12% of leaders say their workforce is truly ready. The technology buyers see the capabilities. The operations leaders see the workflows. They're looking at the same organization and reaching opposite conclusions.

slants.strategy: This CIO-COO gap signals that AI strategy is being set without enough input from the people who run the business day to day. Strategy that doesn't account for operational reality doesn't stick.
slants.operations: You're living in the COO's 7%. The tools are deployed, but the workflows haven't changed. That gap between 'installed' and 'useful' is where your voice matters most.
slants.pm: When an AI rollout stalls, the diagnosis usually starts with the technology. This data says start with the stakeholders instead — are the people who bought the tool and the people who use it even aligned on what success looks like?
slants.admin: If an AI tool feels clunky or unhelpful in your daily work, that's not a you problem — it's a design problem. The 7% vs. 39% gap says the people who chose the tool didn't fully understand how the work actually gets done.

steps: Pick one AI tool your organization has deployed — a copilot, chatbot, automation, or analytics feature.
Rate it yourself from 1 to 5 on two questions: "How ready is this tool for my work?" and "How ready is my work for this tool?"

If the two scores are different, open your AI tool and ask: "I rated an AI tool at work [X]/5 for tool readiness and [Y]/5 for workflow readiness. What's the most common reason for that gap, and what's one practical step to close it?"
Share your two scores and the AI's diagnosis in the group — comparing across roles shows where the real friction lives.

homework.title: This weekend, let AI help you make a decision you've been going back and forth on.
homework.body: Not a life-altering decision — something mid-stakes. Which trip to take. Whether to sign up for that class. What to do about the neighbor's fence. Use any free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity) and try this:
homework.prompt:
"I'm trying to decide between [option A] and [option B]. Before you give me advice, ask me five questions about what matters most to me in this decision. Then score each option against my own criteria and tell me which one wins — and by how much."

The technique here — making the AI surface your criteria before it advises — prevents generic advice. It works for work decisions too, but start with something personal where the stakes are low and the learning is real.

2026-07-12.intro: Sunday. Three ideas to carry into Monday — about what your brain is worth without AI, where the new wage premium lives, and the 57% of your week that isn't really work.

trends.title: Your ability to think without AI is about to become a hiring test
trends.body: Gartner's 2026 Strategic Predictions include a striking forecast: 50% of global organizations will require "AI-free" skills assessments by end of year — driven by concerns that GenAI use is causing critical-thinking atrophy. The logic: as more employees outsource analysis, calculation, and decision-framing to AI, the ability to reason independently becomes rarer. And rarer means more valuable. Organizations aren't banning AI. They're testing whether you can still think without it.

slants.strategy: Your strategic value was never in generating options — AI does that now. It's in evaluating them. The clearer your independent judgment, the more irreplaceable your seat at the table.
slants.operations: Operational problem-solving often requires reasoning under constraints AI doesn't see — political, budgetary, historical. That skill is about to be formally measured, not just assumed.
slants.pm: Project managers who can assess AI-generated plans critically — spotting missing dependencies, unrealistic timelines, hidden assumptions — are the ones who'll stand out in AI-free evaluations.
slants.admin: Every time you read an AI response and think "that's not quite right for this situation" — that's the critical thinking being tested. It's not a technical skill. It's a judgment skill you already have.

steps: Pick a question you'd normally ask AI — a recommendation, a summary, a decision framework.
Before prompting, spend 5 minutes writing your own answer on paper or in a notes app. No tools, no search, just your thinking.
Now ask your company-approved AI tool the same question. Compare the two answers: "Where did my reasoning and the AI's reasoning differ? What did I catch that it missed — or vice versa?"
Share your comparison in the group — the gap between your thinking and AI's thinking is where your value lives.

trends.title: AI-era skills are paying up to 15% more — but not for everyone equally
trends.body: The IMF's January 2026 report on new job creation in the AI age analyzed labor markets across advanced and emerging economies. The finding: job postings requiring new AI-adjacent skills offer wage premiums of 3–15%, with the highest premiums going to roles listing multiple new capabilities. But the benefits aren't evenly distributed — they concentrate at the top and bottom of the skill spectrum, potentially contributing to the shrinking of the middle class.

slants.strategy: The premium isn't for using one AI tool — it's for combining new skills with existing domain expertise. Strategy professionals who layer AI fluency onto industry knowledge sit in the highest-premium zone.
slants.operations: Ops roles that blend process expertise with AI skills — knowing what to automate, what to monitor, what to escalate — are the ones commanding the premium. Pure execution without AI literacy is the zone being compressed.
slants.pm: The 15% premium isn't for knowing how AI works technically — it's for knowing how to manage AI-integrated projects, set realistic expectations, and bridge technical and business teams.
slants.admin: The good news: the premium rewards practical skills, not credentials. Every new AI technique you practice — prompting, output evaluation, workflow design — moves you toward the premium zone.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool and paste this prompt: "I work as a [your role] in [your industry]. Based on current labor market trends, what are the top 3 AI-adjacent skills that would make someone in my position more valuable — not technical coding skills, but practical capabilities I could develop in the next 3 months?"
Review the three skills. For each, rate yourself 1–5 on how confident you feel using it today.

Pick the lowest-rated skill and ask a follow-up: "Give me one specific exercise I could do this week to start building [that skill]. Keep it under 15 minutes and assume I have no technical background."
Try the exercise before Friday and share what you practiced in the group.

trends.title: 57% of your work week is spent talking about work — not doing it
trends.body: Microsoft's 2026 Work Trend Index measured how people actually spend their time across 365 apps: 57% goes to communication — meetings, email, and chat — while only 43% goes to creation. Meanwhile, 68% of workers say they don't have enough uninterrupted focus time. AI meeting tools and scheduling assistants are starting to compress the communication layer, but the real shift isn't automating meetings — it's reclaiming the time to do the work those meetings were supposed to enable.

slants.strategy: If more than half your calendar is communication, the strategic question isn't "how do I get more done?" — it's "which of these meetings could be an async brief that AI drafts for me?"
slants.operations: Every status update meeting that could be an AI-generated dashboard is time returned to actual operations. The 57% number is a starting point for identifying what to cut.
slants.pm: Project managers live in the communication layer by design — but AI can draft status updates, summarize threads, and prep agendas. Reclaim even 20% of that 57% and your delivery capacity changes.
slants.admin: You're often the one scheduling, prepping, and following up on meetings. AI scheduling and summarization tools don't replace your role — they free you from the mechanical parts so you can focus on the judgment parts.

steps: Open your calendar for this past week and estimate: what percentage of your time was communication (meetings, email, chat) versus creation (writing, analyzing, building, deciding)?
Pick the one recurring meeting or communication ritual that consumed the most time relative to its value.

Open your company-approved AI tool and try this: "I have a recurring [meeting/email/process] that takes [X minutes] per week. Its purpose is [describe it]. Suggest three ways I could get the same outcome in less time — including whether AI could handle any part of it asynchronously."
Try one suggestion this week and share what you reclaimed in the group.

homework.title: Tonight, let AI help you plan the week ahead — the personal side.
homework.body: Sunday evenings are when the week's weight starts settling in. Use any free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity) to turn that vague "I have so much to do" feeling into something manageable:
homework.prompt:
"Here's everything on my mind for the week ahead: [brain-dump your list — personal errands, appointments, things you're dreading, things you're looking forward to]. Sort these into three buckets: must-do, want-to-do, and can-wait. For the must-do list, put them in the order I should tackle them and tell me why that sequence makes sense."

The technique — brain-dumping everything before asking AI to organize it — works because getting it out of your head is half the relief. AI handles the sorting; you handle the doing.

2026-07-13.intro: Happy Monday. Three things to start your week with — a compliance deadline that moved, a reminder that most of your skills aren't going anywhere, and a name for that end-of-day fog.

trends.title: Europe's AI compliance clock just reset — but the readiness problem didn't
trends.body: On June 29, the EU Council gave final approval to the AI Act Omnibus, pushing the high-risk AI compliance deadline from August 2, 2026 to December 2, 2027. That's 16 extra months for companies using AI in hiring, credit scoring, law enforcement, and other sensitive areas. But according to the Cloud Security Alliance, 78% of organizations hadn't taken meaningful steps toward the original deadline. The extension buys time — it doesn't buy preparedness.

slants.strategy: If your organization uses AI in hiring, risk scoring, or resource allocation, you now have until late 2027 — but the inventory of what you're actually running should start this quarter.
slants.operations: The new timeline means current AI workflows won't face enforcement yet, but documenting how they work now is far easier than reverse-engineering them under pressure later.
slants.pm: Compliance programs that start 16 months early look like planning. Ones that start 6 months late look like firefighting. This is your window to lead, not chase.
slants.admin: You likely interact with AI-assisted tools daily without knowing which ones fall under the new rules. A simple running list now saves a frantic audit later.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.

Ask: "I work in [your department — strategy, operations, project management, or administration]. What kinds of tasks in my role might involve AI that the EU AI Act classifies as high-risk? Explain in plain language."
Read the answer. Even if your company isn't based in Europe, many global organizations will need to comply — and the categories are broader than you'd expect.
Share the most surprising item on the list in the group.

trends.title: 70% of your current skills still matter in an AI world — they just land differently
trends.body: McKinsey Global Institute's latest workforce report found that more than 70% of the skills employers seek today are used in both automatable and non-automatable work. Most of your skills don't disappear with AI — they shift context. And skills rooted in social and emotional intelligence — negotiation, coaching, conflict resolution — are the ones AI handles worst and may change the least.

slants.strategy: Strategic framing, stakeholder negotiation, and cross-team alignment are skills AI handles poorly. Your playbook isn't obsolete — it's being promoted.
slants.operations: Process knowledge — knowing why a workflow exists, not just what it does — is the piece AI can't learn from documentation alone.
slants.pm: Facilitation, expectation-setting, and knowing when a plan needs a human override get more valuable as AI handles more of the execution.
slants.admin: Institutional knowledge — who to call, when to escalate, what the exec actually means — is a skill set AI can't replicate. Don't undervalue it.

steps: Pick one skill you use every day at work — negotiation, scheduling, writing, analysis, anything.
Open your company-approved AI tool and ask: "What parts of [your skill] can AI handle today, and what parts still require a human? Be specific."
Read the answer and notice where AI draws the line. That boundary is where your durable value lives.
Share the skill you chose and what surprised you in the group.

trends.title: There's a name for that foggy feeling after a day of AI tools: brain fry
trends.body: A BCG study published in Harvard Business Review surveyed 1,488 U.S. workers and found that 14% of AI-using workers experience "brain fry" — mental fog, headaches, and slower decisions from constant AI oversight. The twist: one or two AI tools boost productivity, but at tool number four, it drops. The hardest hit aren't the skeptics — they're the early adopters and high performers who leaned in the most.

slants.strategy: More AI tools in your stack doesn't mean more strategic clarity. If you're reviewing AI output all day, you're editing — not thinking.
slants.operations: Tool consolidation isn't just an IT conversation — it's a productivity one. Fewer, better-integrated tools beats a dozen overlapping dashboards.
slants.pm: When your team uses four different AI tools for overlapping tasks, you're not enabling them — you're fragmenting their attention.
slants.admin: If you end the day foggier than when you started, it's not a personal failing — it might be the tool count. Permission to simplify.

steps: Think about your last full workday. Estimate how many hours you spent reviewing, editing, or double-checking AI-generated output.
Pick one instance where checking the AI's work took longer than doing the task yourself would have.
Ask yourself honestly: "Was the AI actually saving me time here — or adding a step?"
Share your answer in the group. Sometimes the most productive AI move is using fewer AI tools.

homework.title: Tonight, let AI decode something you've been ignoring.
homework.body: That insurance explanation, the odd charge on your statement, the lease clause you've never quite understood — bring it to a free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity):
homework.prompt:
"Here's a document I don't fully understand: [paste or describe it]. Explain it to me in plain language, paragraph by paragraph. Then tell me the one thing in here I should actually pay attention to — and why."

The technique here — asking AI to highlight what matters, not just translate — teaches you to use AI as a filter, not a dictionary.

2026-07-14.intro: Three things for your Tuesday — about the gap between having AI tools and using them, why job descriptions haven't caught up, and who actually trusts AI at work.

trends.title: 85% of workers have AI tools — only 25% are using them
trends.body: IBM's 2026 Global CEO Study found that 85% of employees now have access to AI tools at work, but only 25% use them regularly. That's a 60-point gap between availability and adoption. The barrier isn't resistance — it's confusion. Only 9% of workers say they feel very comfortable using AI, and just 25% say their employer has clearly communicated how AI should be used in their specific role. Abstract training doesn't close the gap. Concrete, role-specific starting points do.

slants.strategy: Your strategic value from AI starts with one repeatable use case, not a grand vision. Find the single workflow where AI saves you 20 minutes and build from there.
slants.operations: Ops teams sit on some of the best use cases — recurring reports, data reconciliation, exception triage — but no one has mapped AI to those specific tasks yet.
slants.pm: If your team has AI access but isn't using it, the blocker is almost never the technology. It's a missing answer to the question: what do I actually do with this on a Tuesday morning?
slants.admin: If you're in the 75% who haven't started, that's not a personal failure — it means the rollout didn't include a clear first step for your role. Today's challenge is that first step.

steps: Pick one task you do at least once a week — a status update, a meeting prep, a document review, a scheduling puzzle.

Open your company-approved AI tool and paste this prompt: "I need to [describe the task in one sentence]. Walk me through exactly how you'd help me do this — step by step, in plain language. Assume I've never used AI for work before."
Read the response. If it's useful, try it on the real task this week. If it misses the mark, reword your description to be more specific and try again.
Share what task you picked and whether the AI's walkthrough was actually helpful in the group.

trends.title: AI tools arrived fast — job descriptions didn't keep up
trends.body: Deloitte's 2026 State of AI in the Enterprise report found that workforce access to AI expanded by 50% in a single year — from fewer than 40% to around 60% of workers now equipped with sanctioned tools. But 84% of companies have not redesigned jobs or workflows around those tools. The training programs arrived. The tool licenses arrived. What didn't arrive: updated success metrics, redefined responsibilities, or clear expectations for how AI fits into daily work.

slants.strategy: If your role's success metrics haven't changed since AI tools arrived, you're being measured on the old job while doing a new one. That's worth a conversation with leadership.
slants.operations: When the SOP says 'compile the report manually' but AI can draft it in seconds, the SOP is the bottleneck — not the tool.
slants.pm: Project scoping still assumes pre-AI timelines for research, drafting, and review. If you're not adjusting estimates for AI-assisted work, you're leaving capacity on the table.
slants.admin: Your job description probably hasn't changed in years, but your actual work has. Documenting what you really do now — including where AI helps — is the first step toward getting that recognized.

steps: Pull up your current job description — or write down your top 5 responsibilities as you understand them.
Next to each one, note whether AI could realistically help with any part of it today — drafting, researching, organizing, summarizing.

Open your company-approved AI tool and ask: "Here are my job responsibilities: [list them]. For each one, suggest one specific way AI could change how I do it — not replace it, but change the process. Be practical, not theoretical."
Compare the AI's suggestions to how you actually work today. Share the biggest gap between your official job description and your real workflow in the group.

trends.title: Trust in AI tracks with your seat at the table — not the technology itself
trends.body: SHRM's 2026 workplace research found a striking pattern: 26% of individual contributors say their trust decreases when leaders rely heavily on AI, compared to just 9% of directors. Meanwhile, 45% of entry-level workers report feeling pressure to adopt AI. The disconnect isn't about the tools — it's about information. People closest to AI decisions are more informed and more trusting. People furthest from leadership hear "use AI" without hearing why, how, or what it won't change about their job.

slants.strategy: If your team doesn't trust AI, the fix isn't better tools — it's better communication about why AI is being introduced and what it means for their roles specifically.
slants.operations: Trust erodes when AI is rolled out as a mandate with no input from the people doing the work. Involving ops teams in the 'how' builds buy-in faster than any training deck.
slants.pm: You often bridge leadership and team. If you're hearing AI skepticism below you, you're in the best position to translate intent from above and concerns from below.
slants.admin: If you feel pressure to use AI but haven't been told why or how it affects your role, that's not your gap — it's a communication gap. You're allowed to ask.

steps: Think about how AI was introduced at your workplace. Were you told why it was being adopted — or just told to use it?

Open your company-approved AI tool and try this: "I want to explain to my team why we're using AI tools — in a way that's honest, not hype. Our team does [describe your team's work]. Write a short explanation (3-4 sentences) that acknowledges real concerns and focuses on what won't change about our roles, not just what will."
Read the output and rewrite it in your own voice. Would you actually send this to your team?
Share your version — or the most honest line from it — in the group.

homework.title: Tonight, let AI help you rehearse a conversation you've been putting off.
homework.body: Most of us have a conversation we're avoiding — asking for something, saying no, giving feedback, setting a boundary. Use any free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity) to practice first:
homework.prompt:
"I need to have a conversation about [describe the situation — keep it vague if you want]. I want to be [direct / kind / firm / honest — pick your tone]. Give me three different ways to open the conversation, ranked from most cautious to most direct. Then tell me which one you'd recommend and why."

The technique here — asking for options ranked along a dimension — helps you see the full range before you pick your approach. You almost never go with the first draft.

2026-07-15.intro: Three for your Wednesday — about where the labor market is splitting, who's carrying the hidden weight of AI adoption, and what happens to your confidence when you let AI do the thinking.

trends.title: The labor market is splitting into two tracks — and only one is accelerating
trends.body: PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, released last week, analyzed over one billion job ads across 27 countries. The finding: AI is creating a two-track labor market. 'Professionalised' roles — where AI handles routine work so humans focus on judgment and expertise — are growing at twice the rate of 'democratised' roles, where AI makes the job easier for non-experts to perform. The professionalised track also shows 42% faster wage growth. The dividing line isn't technical skill. It's whether your role is moving toward deeper judgment or toward broader accessibility.

slants.strategy: Strategy roles sit squarely on the professionalised track — AI handles the data pull, you handle the interpretation. That split is now showing up in pay.
slants.operations: Ops roles that combine process knowledge with AI fluency — knowing what to automate and what to escalate — are landing on the faster-growing track.
slants.pm: Entry-level PM roles exposed to AI are now seven times more likely to require traditionally senior skills like judgment and leadership. The bar is rising, but so is the value.
slants.admin: The democratised track isn't a downgrade — it means more people can do parts of your role, which makes the parts only you can do more visible and more valued.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool and paste this prompt: "I work as a [your role] in [your industry]. Based on the concept of 'professionalised' vs. 'democratised' AI roles, which track is my role on? What are the 2-3 judgment-based skills in my work that AI can't easily replicate?"
Read the response and compare it to your own instinct. Did the AI name skills you take for granted?
Pick one of those judgment-based skills and ask a follow-up: "How could I make this skill more visible to my team and leadership this quarter?"
Share which skill surprised you and your plan to make it visible in the group.

trends.title: AI adoption's hidden cost: the middle manager squeeze
trends.body: Harvard Business Review reported in June that AI adoption is quietly overloading middle managers. Executives use AI to expand scope and accelerate strategy. Junior staff use it for drafting and research. But managers in between are absorbing new duties — validating AI outputs, coaching teams on AI skills, spotting errors — on top of unchanged delivery targets and without formal support. A Harvard Business School study found that 60% of a typical manager's week was already administrative coordination. AI promised to shrink that. Instead, it added a new layer.

slants.strategy: If your strategy depends on AI adoption, the bottleneck isn't the technology or the junior staff — it's the middle layer being asked to make it all work without a revised mandate.
slants.operations: Ops managers are natural integrators. But 'integrate AI into the team' landed on their plate without removing anything else from it.
slants.pm: You're likely the person being asked to validate AI outputs, train the team, and still hit the same deadlines. If that sounds familiar, the problem is structural, not personal.
slants.admin: When a manager is stretched thin by AI validation duties, the admin who can prescreen AI output or flag obvious errors becomes a force multiplier — and that's worth naming.

steps: Think about the last month. Estimate how many hours you spent on AI-related tasks that didn't exist a year ago — reviewing AI drafts, explaining tools to colleagues, double-checking AI output.

Open your company-approved AI tool and try this prompt: "I manage or support a team and I've taken on these new AI-related responsibilities: [list them]. Help me draft a short proposal (3-4 bullet points) for how my organization could formally support this work — through training, adjusted goals, or dedicated time."
Read the output. Edit it until it sounds like something you'd actually share with your manager or leadership.
Post in the group: what's the one AI-related task you've absorbed that nobody officially asked you to do?

trends.title: Letting AI do the thinking is quietly eroding your confidence in your own
trends.body: Research published by the American Psychological Association in April found that relying on AI for work tasks reduces confidence in independent reasoning and perceived ownership of ideas. In a study of 1,923 workers completing simulated planning, analysis, and strategy tasks, 58% agreed that AI "did most of the thinking." Those who passively accepted AI suggestions reported the steepest drops in self-efficacy. The fix wasn't less AI — it was actively modifying or challenging AI output, which preserved both confidence and sense of authorship.

slants.strategy: If you're accepting AI's strategic framing without pushing back, you're not saving time — you're outsourcing the judgment that makes your role valuable.
slants.operations: Ops decisions often require context AI can't see. Every time you override an AI recommendation because you know better, that's your expertise holding the line.
slants.pm: The instinct to accept an AI-generated plan and move on is strong when you're busy. But a plan you didn't shape is a plan you can't defend when it hits resistance.
slants.admin: If you've caught yourself thinking "the AI probably knows better" — pause. You know the people, the politics, and the exceptions. The AI doesn't.

steps: Pick a task you've been using AI for recently — a draft, a summary, a plan, a recommendation.
Before asking AI this time, spend 3 minutes writing your own rough version first. Just bullet points or a quick outline — it doesn't need to be polished.

Now run the same task through your company-approved AI tool. Compare the two: "Where does my version differ from the AI's? What did I include that the AI missed — context, nuance, political awareness?"
Keep your rough version alongside the AI output when you deliver the final product. Share in the group: what did your version catch that the AI didn't?

homework.title: Tonight, practice being the editor — not the audience.
homework.body: The APA research says passive AI use hurts your confidence, but active use builds it. Try this in any free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity):
homework.prompt:
"Help me plan [something personal — a weekend trip, a dinner party, a tough email, a home project]. Give me your best recommendation, and then I'm going to argue with it. After I push back, revise your plan based on my objections."

The technique — deliberately disagreeing with AI before accepting its answer — trains the habit of treating AI as a starting point, not an authority. That habit carries straight into work.

2026-07-16.intro: Thursday. Three data points this week that share a thread: the organizations pulling ahead on AI aren't doing more — they're thinking differently about what AI is for.

trends.title: Most of AI's economic value is going to a small group — and spending isn't what separates them
trends.body: PwC's 2026 AI Performance Study surveyed 1,217 executives across 25 sectors. The headline: 74% of AI's economic value is being captured by just 20% of organizations. The top performers aren't outspending everyone else — they're using AI differently. Companies generating the most value are 2–3 times more likely to use AI for growth and business reinvention than for cost-cutting. The result: a 7.2x ROI gap and operating margins four points higher.

slants.strategy: If your AI business case starts and ends with "reduce costs," you're optimizing for the smaller prize. The top performers are asking where AI creates new revenue — not just where it trims existing spend.
slants.operations: Ops leaders in the top 20% aren't automating the same old processes faster. They're asking which workflows to redesign entirely now that AI is a factor.
slants.pm: If every AI project in your portfolio is a cost-reduction play, the portfolio itself may need rebalancing. Growth-focused AI projects are showing a stronger track record.
slants.admin: The companies getting the most from AI aren't the ones with the most tools — they're the ones with the clearest strategy. That matters when your leadership is still shopping for the next shiny thing.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.

Paste this prompt: "My organization is investing in AI. Help me brainstorm 3 ways AI could create new value in my department — not by cutting costs or reducing headcount, but by enabling something we couldn't do before. My department does [describe your team's work]."
Read the three ideas. Pick the one that feels most realistic for your team this quarter.
Share that idea in the group — and whether 'growth' or 'cost-cutting' is the dominant AI conversation at your organization right now.

trends.title: More knowledge workers are going independent — and AI is the catalyst
trends.body: Upwork's 2026 Future Workforce Index, released this week, surveyed 2,400 U.S. knowledge workers. The finding: 38% of skilled knowledge workers now freelance, up from 28% a year ago. And 58% of full-time employees say they're considering it, up from 36%. AI is the accelerant — freelancers who integrate AI into complex professional work earn 34% more per hour. Upwork calls the emerging archetype the "AI Orchestrator": someone who connects AI tools to domain expertise and takes accountability for the output.

slants.strategy: The rise of skilled freelancing changes how you source expertise. The best analyst for your next initiative won't always be on your payroll — and doesn't need to be.
slants.operations: If 38% of knowledge workers are freelancing, your vendor onboarding, compliance workflows, and access provisioning need to keep pace. Prepare the systems before the demand arrives.
slants.pm: Project staffing is shifting. Knowing how to scope work for a blended team — internal staff plus AI-fluent freelancers — is becoming a core PM skill.
slants.admin: The admin infrastructure around freelancers — contracts, access provisioning, communication channels — is growing. If your organization isn't ready for a larger freelance bench, that gap lands on you first.

steps: Think about the last time your team brought in outside help — a consultant, contractor, or freelancer.

Open your company-approved AI tool and try this prompt: "I work in [your department]. If I could bring in a freelance AI specialist for 10 hours this month, what's the highest-value project I could hand them? Assume they're skilled at using AI for analysis, drafting, and process design."
Read the suggestion. Does it describe work someone on your team is doing slowly — or work nobody has time to start?
Share the project idea and your reaction in the group.

trends.title: AI saves you hours on email — but it can't touch your meeting problem
trends.body: A Harvard Business School AI Institute study tracked 7,000 workers across 66 companies in a randomized controlled trial. Workers with access to AI assistants spent 3.6 fewer hours per week on email — a 31% reduction. Collaborative documents got completed 25% faster. But meetings? No change at all. Same number, same duration, same attendance. The researchers' explanation: email is individual, so one person with AI can change their own habits. Meetings are collective — changing them requires coordination that no individual tool can provide.

slants.strategy: If your AI rollout is saving individuals hours but the meeting calendar hasn't budged, you're reclaiming personal time and losing it again in collective time. The net gain may be smaller than it looks.
slants.operations: This study gives you a clean argument for the meeting audit your team probably needs: AI freed up time, and meetings absorbed it.
slants.pm: You own the meeting cadence. If AI is giving people back 3 hours of email time and that time is vanishing into status syncs, the fix is a project management decision — not a technology one.
slants.admin: You see it before anyone else: calendars haven't gotten lighter, even though inboxes have. If you manage schedules, you have the data to start the conversation.

steps: Look at your calendar for last week. Count the total hours you spent in meetings.
Now estimate how much time you spent on email — and whether that number has changed since AI tools arrived at your organization.

Open your company-approved AI tool and try: "I spent approximately [X] hours in meetings and [Y] hours on email last week. Based on workplace research, which of those is more likely to be reduced by AI tools, and what's one structural change I could propose to reduce the other?"
Share your meeting-to-email ratio and the most useful suggestion in the group.

homework.title: Tonight, let AI help you think through a purchase you've been mulling over.
homework.body: Everyone has one — a new mattress, a different phone plan, a kitchen gadget, a subscription you're not sure about. Instead of reading 47 reviews, try this in any free AI tool (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity):
homework.prompt:
"I'm thinking about buying [the thing]. Before you recommend anything, ask me 5 questions about what matters most to me — budget, features, how I'll use it, deal-breakers. Then give me 3 options ranked by how well they fit my answers."

The technique — having AI interview you before it advises — produces far better answers than dumping your question cold. It works for any decision, personal or professional.

2026-07-17.intro: Today's stories all point to the same shift: AI is maturing from a technology question into a management one — how you pay for it, how you feed it, and which human skills it makes more valuable, not less.

trends.title: The real AI budget question isn't the model, it's the cleanup
trends.body: A McKinsey report out today reframes how leaders should think about agent costs: <cite index="30-1">Agentic AI tasks can use nearly 1,000 times more tokens than conventional code reasoning or chat-based AI tasks, the report said, "Per-token pricing has stopped being a useful measure for what enterprises actually pay for gen AI."</cite> The bigger surprise is where the money actually goes: <cite index="30-9">about 60 per cent an agentic task's costs, in fact, are tied to refining answers</cite>, not generating them. As McKinsey put it, <cite index="30-5">the decision to scale an agent is increasingly becoming a complex and fast-changing economics decision, not a technical one</cite>.

slants.strategy: When you build the AI business case next quarter, budget for revision cycles and retries, not just a per-seat license fee.
slants.operations: Before approving a new agent workflow, ask how much of its cost sits in verification and rework — that's the number that actually predicts your bill.
slants.pm: Bake cost variance into your AI-enabled project plans, since the same task can price out differently depending on how many times the agent has to try again.
slants.admin: The more back-and-forth you do polishing an AI draft, the more it's actually costing behind the scenes — tighter first requests save real money.

steps: Pick one recurring task you already hand to an AI assistant, like a weekly status summary or a meeting recap.
Run it once with a single, specific request instead of your usual back-and-forth: "Draft this in its final, polished form in one attempt — don't wait for me to ask for revisions."
Compare that one-shot result to how many rounds of editing you'd normally do to get the same quality.
Notice which part of the task — the first draft or the endless tweaking — actually took the most effort. That's the expensive part, for you and for the AI.
Share in the group: which task got cheaper (and faster) when you asked for the finished version up front?

trends.title: Your AI pilots are ready to scale — your data isn't
trends.body: McKinsey's tech insights team flagged a quieter constraint behind all the agentic AI headlines: <cite index="4-1">As companies push their AI pilots to scale, data is emerging as a constraint. Leaders are prioritizing data readiness</cite> before adding more automation. Separate industry research backs this up — <cite index="16-18">nearly 60% of AI leaders say legacy integration is a primary adoption challenge when implementing advanced AI like agentic AI</cite>. Translation: the tool isn't the hard part anymore. Getting clean, connected information into it is.

slants.strategy: Add "data readiness" as its own line item in your AI roadmap — it now predicts scale-up success more than the model choice does.
slants.operations: Before rolling an AI pilot out wider, audit whether the systems feeding it are consistent, not just whether the pilot worked in a clean test environment.
slants.pm: Build a data-quality checkpoint into your AI project timeline; assume the integration work takes longer than the AI setup itself.
slants.admin: If an AI tool gives you a confusing or incomplete answer, the problem is often messy source data upstream, not the tool — flag it instead of just re-prompting.

steps: Pick one workflow where you already lean on an AI tool — a report, a dataset, a status doc.
Ask your company-approved AI tool: "List the top 3 places in this workflow where you had to guess, or where the information you received was inconsistent or incomplete."
Read the output and see if any of those gaps are things you already suspected but hadn't named.
Pick one gap and flag it to whoever owns that data or process — a five-minute heads-up now saves a bigger headache later.
Share in the group what surprised you most about where the messiness lives.

trends.title: AI is making junior jobs demand senior judgment
trends.body: PwC's newest global jobs analysis found a real divide opening up in how AI reshapes careers. <cite index="24-1">Analysis of US data shows AI-exposed entry-level roles are seven times more likely to require traditionally senior-level skills such as judgement and leadership. These roles grew 35% since 2019, while other entry-level roles declined by 10%</cite>. As the report put it, <cite index="24-6,24-7">the traditional relationship between experience and expertise is changing. AI is removing some of the routine work that once acted as an apprenticeship, while increasing demand for judgement, leadership and adaptability much earlier in careers</cite>.

slants.strategy: Rethink your talent pipeline assumptions — the old model of "years in seat before judgment calls" is compressing fast, and your planning cycles should account for it.
slants.operations: Look for the operational tasks AI has already absorbed on your team, then build in earlier, structured chances for junior staff to practice the judgment calls that remain.
slants.pm: When staffing projects, weigh a junior team member's decision-making instincts as heavily as their tool proficiency — that's the skill AI isn't replacing.
slants.admin: The routine parts of your role that AI now handles free up time for the parts that actually build your reputation: judgment, discretion, and knowing what matters.

steps: Pick one task in your role that AI now does quickly for you — drafting, summarizing, formatting, scheduling.
Ask your company-approved AI tool: "Given that AI now handles this task quickly, what judgment or leadership skill becomes more valuable for me to strengthen next?"
Read the suggestion and think of one real moment this week where that skill would have mattered.
Write down one small way to practice that skill in the next two weeks.
Share in the group what skill came up — you might spot a pattern across the team.

homework.title: Let AI plan your next weekend getaway
homework.body: Pick a free tool — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity — and use it to plan a short trip or day out you've been meaning to take. This time, instead of giving it all the details up front, make it work for the answer.
homework.prompt: Try this: "I want to plan a weekend trip but haven't picked a destination. Before you suggest anything, ask me questions one at a time about my budget, interests, and how much driving or flying I'm willing to do. Then give me your top 3 ranked recommendations with a one-line reason for each." Notice how asking it to question you first — instead of dumping everything at once — gets you a sharper, more personal answer.

2026-07-18.intro: Today's drop is about where AI decisions actually get made: which workflows leadership funds, which tools employees quietly reach for anyway, and which skills your team needs to protect on purpose.

trends.title: Companies are done crowdsourcing AI pilots — and starting to pick winners
trends.body: PwC's 2026 predictions call out a pattern most large companies fell into: letting every team run its own AI experiment and hoping something sticks. <cite index="41-6">Crowdsourcing AI efforts can create impressive adoption numbers, but it seldom produces meaningful business outcomes.</cite> The shift now is toward a smaller, sharper approach where <cite index="41-1,41-2">senior leadership picks the spots for focused AI investments, looking for a few key workflows or business processes where payoffs from AI can be big</cite> (PwC, 2026).

slants.strategy: This is your job now — picking the two or three workflows worth a real AI bet, and building the business case for why the rest wait.
slants.operations: Expect fewer scattered pilots landing on your desk and more concentrated, well-resourced rollouts with a clear ROI target attached.
slants.pm: You'll likely run one deep AI project instead of five shallow ones — plan for more scrutiny and higher stakes on the project you do get.
slants.admin: The 'try whatever tool you like' era may be winding down as your org standardizes around a chosen few — get familiar with the frontrunners early.

steps: Open your company-approved AI tool.
Prompt it: "List the 3 recurring tasks on my team that eat the most time each week and have the clearest, most measurable outcome."
Pick the one that feels most winnable — not the flashiest, the most measurable.
Follow up with: "Write a 3-sentence pitch for why this task should be our team's one focused AI project this quarter."
Read the pitch, tighten anything that sounds off, and share it in the group.

trends.title: Employees trust their own AI judgment more than their company's rules
trends.body: A 2026 PagerDuty survey of 1,250 office professionals found <cite index="21-8">two-thirds (66%) of workers who have used AI say they did so even though they believed it was not permitted under company policy</cite>. Even more striking: <cite index="21-1">a majority of office professionals (72%) believe they understand how to use AI for their job better than the team responsible for managing AI at their company</cite> (PagerDuty, June 2026).

slants.strategy: If two-thirds of your workforce is quietly using unapproved tools, your AI budget conversations are missing a huge piece of real usage data.
slants.operations: The workflow shortcuts your team found using personal AI tools are free signal — they show exactly where the approved tools fall short.
slants.pm: If your project plans assume everyone sticks to sanctioned tools only, you may be underestimating both your team's real capacity and its real risk.
slants.admin: Whatever time you're quietly saving with a personal AI tool is worth surfacing to your manager or IT — better to name it than hide it.

steps: Jot down 2-3 AI tools you personally use for work tasks, whether officially approved or not.
Open your company-approved AI tool and prompt: "Here are tasks I do weekly: [list your tasks]. Which of these could I realistically move to an approved tool instead of a personal one?"
Read the suggestions honestly — don't dismiss them just because switching feels inconvenient.
Note one task where you'd still reach for another tool, and ask yourself if that's a real capability gap or just habit.
Share your honest answer in the group — this works best when people are candid, not defensive.

trends.title: Half of executives already see their teams' thinking skills slipping
trends.body: BCG's June 2026 research names a real organizational risk: <cite index="31-20">in a global study of 70 C-suite leaders and senior executives across multiple industries, we found that half are already observing de-skilling in their organizations, and more than 60% believe that de-skilling will pose a material threat within the next three to five years.</cite> One fix already in motion: <cite index="31-1">at CNIL, France's data protection authority, managers are responsible for assessing employees' ability to challenge AI outputs — not just to use them</cite>, making that judgment a visible, evaluated part of the job.

slants.strategy: Build skill-preservation checkpoints into your AI rollout plans now — the gap shows up later as a bad forecast nobody caught in time.
slants.operations: Map which of your processes now depend so heavily on AI that no one on the team could run them manually if the system went down.
slants.pm: Add a human-only checkpoint to at least one project phase per cycle so your team's judgment gets exercised, not just outsourced.
slants.admin: Keep practicing your own drafting and proofreading instincts on at least a few tasks a week — don't let AI polish become your only quality check.

steps: Pick one recurring task you now complete almost entirely with AI help.
Do it once this week without AI, and time yourself.
Open your company-approved AI tool and prompt: "Ask me questions before answering: help me spot 2 judgment calls in [describe the task] where I should double-check an AI's output before trusting it."
Compare your unaided attempt to the AI's usual output — note where your instincts were sharper or shakier than you expected.
Share one honest takeaway in the group.

homework.title: Plan a weekend getaway without doing all the legwork yourself
homework.body: Pick a place you've been meaning to visit and use ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Perplexity to help you plan it — but make the tool work for the details, not just hand you a generic list.
homework.prompt: Try this: "I want to plan a 2-day trip to [destination] on a budget of [amount]. Before you suggest anything, ask me questions about my travel style, food preferences, and pace so your plan actually fits me." Notice how the questions the AI asks change the quality of what it gives you back — that's the habit worth keeping for bigger prompts later.
